### 698. TCP4-only address family capability (V4 supported, V6 unsupported)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/net/connection/uefi/tcp.rs:1-112`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: newtype

**Description**: The API implicitly requires IPv4 addresses; IPv6 inputs trigger `unsupported()` at runtime. This is an invariant about what address families are acceptable for construction/configuration, but it is not encoded in the types of `connect()`/`bind()` which accept `&SocketAddr` (either V4 or V6).

**Evidence**:

```rust
use crate::sys::pal::{helpers, unsupported};
use crate::time::Duration;

pub(crate) enum Tcp {
    V4(tcp4::Tcp4),
}

// SAFETY: UEFI has no threads.
unsafe impl Send for Tcp {}

impl Tcp {
    pub(crate) fn connect(addr: &SocketAddr, timeout: Option<Duration>) -> io::Result<Self> {
        match addr {
            SocketAddr::V4(x) => {
                let temp = tcp4::Tcp4::new()?;
                temp.configure(true, Some(x), None)?;
                temp.connect(timeout)?;
                Ok(Tcp::V4(temp))
            }
            SocketAddr::V6(_) => unsupported(),
        }
    }

    pub(crate) fn bind(addr: &SocketAddr) -> io::Result<Self> {
        match addr {
            SocketAddr::V4(x) => {
                let temp = tcp4::Tcp4::new()?;
                temp.configure(false, None, Some(x))?;
                Ok(Tcp::V4(temp))
            }
            SocketAddr::V6(_) => unsupported(),
        }
    }

    pub(crate) fn accept(&self) -> io::Result<Self> {
        match self {
            Self::V4(client) => client.accept().map(Tcp::V4),
        }
    }

    pub(crate) fn write(&self, buf: &[u8], timeout: Option<Duration>) -> io::Result<usize> {
        match self {
            Self::V4(client) => client.write(buf, timeout),
        }
    }

    pub(crate) fn write_vectored(
        &self,
        buf: &[IoSlice<'_>],
        timeout: Option<Duration>,
    ) -> io::Result<usize> {
        match self {
            Self::V4(client) => client.write_vectored(buf, timeout),
        }
    }

    pub(crate) fn read(&self, buf: &mut [u8], timeout: Option<Duration>) -> io::Result<usize> {
        match self {
            Self::V4(client) => client.read(buf, timeout),
        }
    }

    pub(crate) fn read_vectored(
        &self,
        buf: &mut [IoSliceMut<'_>],
        timeout: Option<Duration>,
    ) -> io::Result<usize> {
        match self {
            Self::V4(client) => client.read_vectored(buf, timeout),
        }
    }

    pub(crate) fn ttl(&self) -> io::Result<u32> {
        match self {
            Self::V4(client) => client.get_mode_data().map(|x| x.time_to_live.into()),
        }
    }

    pub(crate) fn nodelay(&self) -> io::Result<bool> {
        match self {
            Self::V4(client) => {
                let temp = client.get_mode_data()?;
                match NonNull::new(temp.control_option) {
                    Some(x) => unsafe { Ok(x.as_ref().enable_nagle.into()) },
                    None => unsupported(),
                }
            }
        }
    }

    pub fn peer_addr(&self) -> io::Result<SocketAddr> {
        match self {
            Self::V4(client) => client.get_mode_data().map(|x| {
                SocketAddr::new(
                    helpers::ipv4_from_r_efi(x.access_point.remote_address).into(),
                    x.access_point.remote_port,
                )
            }),
        }
    }

    pub fn socket_addr(&self) -> io::Result<SocketAddr> {
        match self {
            Self::V4(client) => client.get_mode_data().map(|x| {
                SocketAddr::new(
                    helpers::ipv4_from_r_efi(x.access_point.station_address).into(),
                    x.access_point.station_port,
                )
            }),
        }
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Tcp

**States:** V4Capable, V6Unsupported

**Transitions:**
- (construction) requires V4Capable input: SocketAddr::V4 -> Tcp::V4; SocketAddr::V6 -> unsupported()

**Evidence:** enum Tcp { V4(tcp4::Tcp4) } encodes only an IPv4 backend; Tcp::connect(): `SocketAddr::V6(_) => unsupported(),` is a runtime rejection of IPv6; Tcp::bind(): `SocketAddr::V6(_) => unsupported(),` same invariant for binding

**Implementation:** Change constructors to accept `&SocketAddrV4` (or a local `Ipv4SocketAddr` newtype) so IPv6 cannot be passed. Alternatively, make the enum include a V6 variant when implemented; until then, keep the type-level restriction at the boundary.

### 880. DirBuilder path precondition (verbatim/Windows-extended path conversion before WinAPI call)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/fs/windows.rs:1-24`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: newtype

**Description**: DirBuilder::mkdir() relies on a precondition that the input Path must be converted/validated into a Windows-compatible (possibly verbatim/extended-length) path buffer before calling CreateDirectoryW. This is enforced only by calling maybe_verbatim(p)? at runtime; the type system does not distinguish a raw &Path from a 'Windows-ready wide path' representation, so other call sites could forget this conversion or attempt to call WinAPI with an unconverted path.

**Evidence**:

```rust
// Note: Other parts of this module contain: struct File, 6 free function(s), impl Send for FindNextFileHandle (0 methods), impl Sync for FindNextFileHandle (0 methods), impl Drop for FindNextFileHandle (1 methods), impl File (37 methods), impl AsInner < Handle > for File (1 methods), impl IntoInner < Handle > for File (1 methods), impl FromInner < Handle > for File (1 methods), impl AsHandle for File (1 methods), impl AsRawHandle for File (1 methods), impl IntoRawHandle for File (1 methods), impl FromRawHandle for File (1 methods), impl FileAttr (14 methods), impl From < c :: WIN32_FIND_DATAW > for FileAttr (1 methods), impl FilePermissions (2 methods), impl FileTimes (3 methods), impl FileType (6 methods); struct FileAttr; struct FileType; struct ReadDir, 1 free function(s), impl Iterator for ReadDir (1 methods); struct FindNextFileHandle; struct DirEntry, impl DirEntry (5 methods); struct OpenOptions, impl Send for OpenOptions (0 methods), impl Sync for OpenOptions (0 methods), impl OpenOptions (20 methods); struct FilePermissions; struct FileTimes; struct DirBuff, impl DirBuff (4 methods), impl AsRef < [MaybeUninit < u8 >] > for DirBuff (1 methods), impl DirBuffIter < 'a > (1 methods), impl Iterator for DirBuffIter < 'a > (1 methods); struct DirBuffIter; enum ReparsePoint, impl ReparsePoint (1 methods); 17 free function(s)

}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct DirBuilder;


// ... (other code) ...

    }
}

impl DirBuilder {
    pub fn new() -> DirBuilder {
        DirBuilder
    }

    pub fn mkdir(&self, p: &Path) -> io::Result<()> {
        let p = maybe_verbatim(p)?;
        cvt(unsafe { c::CreateDirectoryW(p.as_ptr(), ptr::null_mut()) })?;
        Ok(())
    }
}

```

**Entity:** DirBuilder

**States:** UncheckedPath, VerbatimConvertedPath

**Transitions:**
- UncheckedPath -> VerbatimConvertedPath via maybe_verbatim(p)?

**Evidence:** method DirBuilder::mkdir(&self, p: &Path): accepts an unchecked &Path; call: let p = maybe_verbatim(p)?; — runtime conversion/validation step required before WinAPI usage; call: unsafe { c::CreateDirectoryW(p.as_ptr(), ptr::null_mut()) } — requires p to be a valid wide string pointer for Windows API

**Implementation:** Introduce a newtype (e.g., struct WinPathBuf(/* wide */);) returned by maybe_verbatim and accepted by low-level WinAPI wrappers. Make CreateDirectoryW wrapper take &WinPathBuf (or a WinPath<'a>) so callers cannot pass a raw &Path without conversion.

### 960. Stdout validity depends on ambient availability of FD_STDOUT (capability via with_std_fd)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/stdio/sgx.rs:1-37`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: Stdout is a zero-sized handle with no internal state, but every I/O operation implicitly requires that the ambient stdout file descriptor (abi::FD_STDOUT) exists and is usable. This precondition is enforced only at runtime via with_std_fd(...) returning an io::Result; the type system does not encode whether stdout is actually available/initialized for the current environment/phase, nor does it prevent calling write/flush when stdout is unavailable.

**Evidence**:

```rust
// Note: Other parts of this module contain: struct Stdin, impl Stdin (1 methods), impl io :: Read for Stdin (4 methods); struct Stderr, impl Stderr (1 methods), impl io :: Write for Stderr (4 methods); 3 free function(s)

use crate::sys::fd::FileDesc;

pub struct Stdin;
pub struct Stdout;
pub struct Stderr;

// ... (other code) ...

    }
}

impl Stdout {
    pub const fn new() -> Stdout {
        Stdout
    }
}

impl io::Write for Stdout {
    fn write(&mut self, buf: &[u8]) -> io::Result<usize> {
        with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDOUT, |fd| fd.write(buf))
    }

    fn flush(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> {
        with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDOUT, |fd| fd.flush())
    }

    fn write_vectored(&mut self, bufs: &[IoSlice<'_>]) -> io::Result<usize> {
        with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDOUT, |fd| fd.write_vectored(bufs))
    }

    #[inline]
    fn is_write_vectored(&self) -> bool {
        true
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Stdout

**States:** Stdout FD available/valid, Stdout FD unavailable/invalid

**Transitions:**
- (external) unavailable -> available via environment/stdio initialization (not represented in Stdout type)
- (external) available -> unavailable via environment/stdio teardown or fd invalidation (not represented in Stdout type)

**Evidence:** pub struct Stdout; (no fields) — cannot encode whether FD_STDOUT is valid; Stdout::new() -> Stdout constructs unconditionally with no checks; io::Write::write(): with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDOUT, |fd| fd.write(buf)) — relies on global FD_STDOUT and runtime Result for validity; io::Write::flush(): with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDOUT, |fd| fd.flush()) — same implicit precondition; io::Write::write_vectored(): with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDOUT, |fd| fd.write_vectored(bufs)) — same implicit precondition

**Implementation:** Make access to Stdout require a capability/token produced by an initialization step, e.g., `fn stdout(token: &StdIoToken) -> Stdout<Available>`; only `Stdout<Available>: Write`. The `with_std_fd` closure could take/borrow a `StdoutFd` newtype/capability that can only be constructed when FD_STDOUT is known-valid, preventing use before initialization or after teardown at compile time.

### 4. Option value-presence precondition (Some required for expect/unwrap)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/core/src/option.rs:1-439`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: newtype

**Description**: `Option<T>` has two logical states (`None` and `Some(T)`), but methods like `unwrap()` and `expect(msg)` impose a runtime precondition: they are only valid to call when the value is `Some`. Violating the precondition triggers a panic (not a typed error), meaning the caller must uphold the 'is Some' proof out-of-band (by checks, control flow, or domain knowledge). The type system does not provide a way to carry a proven-nonempty `Option` into a context where `unwrap/expect` becomes statically safe.

**Evidence**:

```rust

#[allow(clippy::derived_hash_with_manual_eq)] // PartialEq is manually implemented equivalently
pub enum Option<T> {
    /// No value.
    #[lang = "None"]
    #[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
    None,
    /// Some value of type `T`.
    #[lang = "Some"]
    #[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
    Some(#[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")] T),

// Type implementation
/////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

impl<T> Option<T> {
    /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
    // Querying the contained values
    /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

    /// Returns `true` if the option is a [`Some`] value.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```
    /// let x: Option<u32> = Some(2);
    /// assert_eq!(x.is_some(), true);
    ///
    /// let x: Option<u32> = None;
    /// assert_eq!(x.is_some(), false);
    /// ```
    #[must_use = "if you intended to assert that this has a value, consider `.unwrap()` instead"]
    #[inline]
    #[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
    #[rustc_const_stable(feature = "const_option_basics", since = "1.48.0")]
    pub const fn is_some(&self) -> bool {
        matches!(*self, Some(_))
    }

    /// Returns `true` if the option is a [`Some`] and the value inside of it matches a predicate.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```
    /// let x: Option<u32> = Some(2);
    /// assert_eq!(x.is_some_and(|x| x > 1), true);
    ///
    /// let x: Option<u32> = Some(0);
    /// assert_eq!(x.is_some_and(|x| x > 1), false);
    ///
    /// let x: Option<u32> = None;
    /// assert_eq!(x.is_some_and(|x| x > 1), false);
    ///
    /// let x: Option<String> = Some("ownership".to_string());
    /// assert_eq!(x.as_ref().is_some_and(|x| x.len() > 1), true);
    /// println!("still alive {:?}", x);
    /// ```
    #[must_use]
    #[inline]
    #[stable(feature = "is_some_and", since = "1.70.0")]
    #[rustc_const_unstable(feature = "const_option_ops", issue = "143956")]
    pub const fn is_some_and(self, f: impl [const] FnOnce(T) -> bool + [const] Destruct) -> bool {
        match self {
            None => false,
            Some(x) => f(x),
        }
    }

    /// Returns `true` if the option is a [`None`] value.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```
    /// let x: Option<u32> = Some(2);
    /// assert_eq!(x.is_none(), false);
    ///
    /// let x: Option<u32> = None;
    /// assert_eq!(x.is_none(), true);
    /// ```
    #[must_use = "if you intended to assert that this doesn't have a value, consider \
                  wrapping this in an `assert!()` instead"]
    #[inline]
    #[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
    #[rustc_const_stable(feature = "const_option_basics", since = "1.48.0")]
    pub const fn is_none(&self) -> bool {
        !self.is_some()
    }

    /// Returns `true` if the option is a [`None`] or the value inside of it matches a predicate.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```
    /// let x: Option<u32> = Some(2);
    /// assert_eq!(x.is_none_or(|x| x > 1), true);
    ///
    /// let x: Option<u32> = Some(0);
    /// assert_eq!(x.is_none_or(|x| x > 1), false);
    ///
    /// let x: Option<u32> = None;
    /// assert_eq!(x.is_none_or(|x| x > 1), true);
    ///
    /// let x: Option<String> = Some("ownership".to_string());
    /// assert_eq!(x.as_ref().is_none_or(|x| x.len() > 1), true);
    /// println!("still alive {:?}", x);
    /// ```
    #[must_use]
    #[inline]
    #[stable(feature = "is_none_or", since = "1.82.0")]
    #[rustc_const_unstable(feature = "const_option_ops", issue = "143956")]
    pub const fn is_none_or(self, f: impl [const] FnOnce(T) -> bool + [const] Destruct) -> bool {
        match self {
            None => true,
            Some(x) => f(x),
        }
    }

    /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
    // Adapter for working with references
    /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

    /// Converts from `&Option<T>` to `Option<&T>`.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// Calculates the length of an <code>Option<[String]></code> as an <code>Option<[usize]></code>
    /// without moving the [`String`]. The [`map`] method takes the `self` argument by value,
    /// consuming the original, so this technique uses `as_ref` to first take an `Option` to a
    /// reference to the value inside the original.
    ///
    /// [`map`]: Option::map
    /// [String]: ../../std/string/struct.String.html "String"
    /// [`String`]: ../../std/string/struct.String.html "String"
    ///
    /// ```
    /// let text: Option<String> = Some("Hello, world!".to_string());
    /// // First, cast `Option<String>` to `Option<&String>` with `as_ref`,
    /// // then consume *that* with `map`, leaving `text` on the stack.
    /// let text_length: Option<usize> = text.as_ref().map(|s| s.len());
    /// println!("still can print text: {text:?}");
    /// ```
    #[inline]
    #[rustc_const_stable(feature = "const_option_basics", since = "1.48.0")]
    #[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
    pub const fn as_ref(&self) -> Option<&T> {
        match *self {
            Some(ref x) => Some(x),
            None => None,
        }
    }

    /// Converts from `&mut Option<T>` to `Option<&mut T>`.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```
    /// let mut x = Some(2);
    /// match x.as_mut() {
    ///     Some(v) => *v = 42,
    ///     None => {},
    /// }
    /// assert_eq!(x, Some(42));
    /// ```
    #[inline]
    #[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
    #[rustc_const_stable(feature = "const_option", since = "1.83.0")]
    pub const fn as_mut(&mut self) -> Option<&mut T> {
        match *self {
            Some(ref mut x) => Some(x),
            None => None,
        }
    }

    /// Converts from <code>[Pin]<[&]Option\<T>></code> to <code>Option<[Pin]<[&]T>></code>.
    ///
    /// [&]: reference "shared reference"
    #[inline]
    #[must_use]
    #[stable(feature = "pin", since = "1.33.0")]
    #[rustc_const_stable(feature = "const_option_ext", since = "1.84.0")]
    pub const fn as_pin_ref(self: Pin<&Self>) -> Option<Pin<&T>> {
        // FIXME(const-hack): use `map` once that is possible
        match Pin::get_ref(self).as_ref() {
            // SAFETY: `x` is guaranteed to be pinned because it comes from `self`
            // which is pinned.
            Some(x) => unsafe { Some(Pin::new_unchecked(x)) },
            None => None,
        }
    }

    /// Converts from <code>[Pin]<[&mut] Option\<T>></code> to <code>Option<[Pin]<[&mut] T>></code>.
    ///
    /// [&mut]: reference "mutable reference"
    #[inline]
    #[must_use]
    #[stable(feature = "pin", since = "1.33.0")]
    #[rustc_const_stable(feature = "const_option_ext", since = "1.84.0")]
    pub const fn as_pin_mut(self: Pin<&mut Self>) -> Option<Pin<&mut T>> {
        // SAFETY: `get_unchecked_mut` is never used to move the `Option` inside `self`.
        // `x` is guaranteed to be pinned because it comes from `self` which is pinned.
        unsafe {
            // FIXME(const-hack): use `map` once that is possible
            match Pin::get_unchecked_mut(self).as_mut() {
                Some(x) => Some(Pin::new_unchecked(x)),
                None => None,
            }
        }
    }

    #[inline]
    const fn len(&self) -> usize {
        // Using the intrinsic avoids emitting a branch to get the 0 or 1.
        let discriminant: isize = crate::intrinsics::discriminant_value(self);
        discriminant as usize
    }

    /// Returns a slice of the contained value, if any. If this is `None`, an
    /// empty slice is returned. This can be useful to have a single type of
    /// iterator over an `Option` or slice.
    ///
    /// Note: Should you have an `Option<&T>` and wish to get a slice of `T`,
    /// you can unpack it via `opt.map_or(&[], std::slice::from_ref)`.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```rust
    /// assert_eq!(
    ///     [Some(1234).as_slice(), None.as_slice()],
    ///     [&[1234][..], &[][..]],
    /// );
    /// ```
    ///
    /// The inverse of this function is (discounting
    /// borrowing) [`[_]::first`](slice::first):
    ///
    /// ```rust
    /// for i in [Some(1234_u16), None] {
    ///     assert_eq!(i.as_ref(), i.as_slice().first());
    /// }
    /// ```
    #[inline]
    #[must_use]
    #[stable(feature = "option_as_slice", since = "1.75.0")]
    #[rustc_const_stable(feature = "const_option_ext", since = "1.84.0")]
    pub const fn as_slice(&self) -> &[T] {
        // SAFETY: When the `Option` is `Some`, we're using the actual pointer
        // to the payload, with a length of 1, so this is equivalent to
        // `slice::from_ref`, and thus is safe.
        // When the `Option` is `None`, the length used is 0, so to be safe it
        // just needs to be aligned, which it is because `&self` is aligned and
        // the offset used is a multiple of alignment.
        //
        // Here we assume that `offset_of!` always returns an offset to an
        // in-bounds and correctly aligned position for a `T` (even if in the
        // `None` case it's just padding).
        unsafe {
            slice::from_raw_parts(
                (self as *const Self).byte_add(core::mem::offset_of!(Self, Some.0)).cast(),
                self.len(),
            )
        }
    }

    /// Returns a mutable slice of the contained value, if any. If this is
    /// `None`, an empty slice is returned. This can be useful to have a
    /// single type of iterator over an `Option` or slice.
    ///
    /// Note: Should you have an `Option<&mut T>` instead of a
    /// `&mut Option<T>`, which this method takes, you can obtain a mutable
    /// slice via `opt.map_or(&mut [], std::slice::from_mut)`.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```rust
    /// assert_eq!(
    ///     [Some(1234).as_mut_slice(), None.as_mut_slice()],
    ///     [&mut [1234][..], &mut [][..]],
    /// );
    /// ```
    ///
    /// The result is a mutable slice of zero or one items that points into
    /// our original `Option`:
    ///
    /// ```rust
    /// let mut x = Some(1234);
    /// x.as_mut_slice()[0] += 1;
    /// assert_eq!(x, Some(1235));
    /// ```
    ///
    /// The inverse of this method (discounting borrowing)
    /// is [`[_]::first_mut`](slice::first_mut):
    ///
    /// ```rust
    /// assert_eq!(Some(123).as_mut_slice().first_mut(), Some(&mut 123))
    /// ```
    #[inline]
    #[must_use]
    #[stable(feature = "option_as_slice", since = "1.75.0")]
    #[rustc_const_stable(feature = "const_option_ext", since = "1.84.0")]
    pub const fn as_mut_slice(&mut self) -> &mut [T] {
        // SAFETY: When the `Option` is `Some`, we're using the actual pointer
        // to the payload, with a length of 1, so this is equivalent to
        // `slice::from_mut`, and thus is safe.
        // When the `Option` is `None`, the length used is 0, so to be safe it
        // just needs to be aligned, which it is because `&self` is aligned and
        // the offset used is a multiple of alignment.
        //
        // In the new version, the intrinsic creates a `*const T` from a
        // mutable reference  so it is safe to cast back to a mutable pointer
        // here. As with `as_slice`, the intrinsic always returns a pointer to
        // an in-bounds and correctly aligned position for a `T` (even if in
        // the `None` case it's just padding).
        unsafe {
            slice::from_raw_parts_mut(
                (self as *mut Self).byte_add(core::mem::offset_of!(Self, Some.0)).cast(),
                self.len(),
            )
        }
    }

    /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
    // Getting to contained values
    /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

    /// Returns the contained [`Some`] value, consuming the `self` value.
    ///
    /// # Panics
    ///
    /// Panics if the value is a [`None`] with a custom panic message provided by
    /// `msg`.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```
    /// let x = Some("value");
    /// assert_eq!(x.expect("fruits are healthy"), "value");
    /// ```
    ///
    /// ```should_panic
    /// let x: Option<&str> = None;
    /// x.expect("fruits are healthy"); // panics with `fruits are healthy`
    /// ```
    ///
    /// # Recommended Message Style
    ///
    /// We recommend that `expect` messages are used to describe the reason you
    /// _expect_ the `Option` should be `Some`.
    ///
    /// ```should_panic
    /// # let slice: &[u8] = &[];
    /// let item = slice.get(0)
    ///     .expect("slice should not be empty");
    /// ```
    ///
    /// **Hint**: If you're having trouble remembering how to phrase expect
    /// error messages remember to focus on the word "should" as in "env
    /// variable should be set by blah" or "the given binary should be available
    /// and executable by the current user".
    ///
    /// For more detail on expect message styles and the reasoning behind our
    /// recommendation please refer to the section on ["Common Message
    /// Styles"](../../std/error/index.html#common-message-styles) in the [`std::error`](../../std/error/index.html) module docs.
    #[inline]
    #[track_caller]
    #[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
    #[rustc_diagnostic_item = "option_expect"]
    #[rustc_allow_const_fn_unstable(const_precise_live_drops)]
    #[rustc_const_stable(feature = "const_option", since = "1.83.0")]
    pub const fn expect(self, msg: &str) -> T {
        match self {
            Some(val) => val,
            None => expect_failed(msg),
        }
    }

    /// Returns the contained [`Some`] value, consuming the `self` value.
    ///
    /// Because this function may panic, its use is generally discouraged.
    /// Panics are meant for unrecoverable errors, and
    /// [may abort the entire program][panic-abort].
    ///
    /// Instead, prefer to use pattern matching and handle the [`None`]
    /// case explicitly, or call [`unwrap_or`], [`unwrap_or_else`], or
    /// [`unwrap_or_default`]. In functions returning `Option`, you can use
    /// [the `?` (try) operator][try-option].
    ///
    /// [panic-abort]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch09-01-unrecoverable-errors-with-panic.html
    /// [try-option]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch09-02-recoverable-errors-with-result.html#where-the--operator-can-be-used
    /// [`unwrap_or`]: Option::unwrap_or
    /// [`unwrap_or_else`]: Option::unwrap_or_else
    /// [`unwrap_or_default`]: Option::unwrap_or_default
    ///
    /// # Panics
    ///
    /// Panics if the self value equals [`None`].
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```
    /// let x = Some("air");
    /// assert_eq!(x.unwrap(), "air");
    /// ```
    ///
    /// ```should_panic
    /// let x: Option<&str> = None;
    /// assert_eq!(x.unwrap(), "air"); // fails
    /// ```
    #[inline(always)]
    #[track_caller]
    #[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
    #[rustc_diagnostic_item = "option_unwrap"]
    #[rustc_allow_const_fn_unstable(const_precise_live_drops)]
    #[rustc_const_stable(feature = "const_option", since = "1.83.0")]
    pub const fn unwrap(self) -> T {
        match self {
            Some(val) => val,
            None => unwrap_failed(),
        }
    }

    /// Returns the contained [`Some`] value or a provided default.
    ///
    /// Arguments passed to `unwrap_or` are eagerly evaluated; if you are passing
    /// the result of a function call, it is recommended to use [`unwrap_or_else`],
    /// which is lazily evaluated.
    ///
    /// [`unwrap_or_else`]: Option::unwrap_or_else
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```
    /// assert_eq!(Some("car").unwrap_or(
// ... (truncated) ...
```

**Entity:** Option<T> (methods: expect/unwrap)

**States:** None, Some(T)

**Transitions:**
- Some(T) -> T via unwrap()
- Some(T) -> T via expect(msg)

**Evidence:** enum Option<T> { None, Some(T) } encodes presence/absence as runtime state; method `pub const fn expect(self, msg: &str) -> T`: `None => expect_failed(msg)` (panic path) and docs: "Panics if the value is a None"; method `pub const fn unwrap(self) -> T`: `None => unwrap_failed()` (panic path) and docs: "Panics if the self value equals None"; method `is_some(&self) -> bool` exists as a runtime query but does not create a compile-time proof usable by `unwrap/expect`

**Implementation:** Introduce a wrapper like `struct SomeValue<T>(T);` (or a crate-level `NonNone<Option<T>>`) constructed only from `Option<T>` via `TryFrom`/`Result`, and implement `into_inner(self) -> T` without panicking. In std this is essentially a `Result`-based API (`ok_or(...)`) but a newtype can carry the proven invariant across API boundaries so `unwrap`-like extraction is total.

### 527. Platform-dependent time availability (Supported vs NotSupported fields)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/fs.rs:1-271`

**Confidence**: medium

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: `Metadata::{modified,accessed,created}` are only valid on platforms/filesystems where the respective timestamp is available. The API exposes this as a runtime `io::Result<SystemTime>` and documentation warnings; callers must handle `Err` to avoid assuming the field exists. This is an implicit capability/availability invariant (per platform and sometimes per filesystem) that cannot be checked at compile time in the current API because `Metadata` does not encode which time fields are supported.

**Evidence**:

impl Metadata {
    /// Returns the file type for this metadata.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```no_run
    /// fn main() -> std::io::Result<()> {
    ///     use std::fs;
    ///
    ///     let metadata = fs::metadata("foo.txt")?;
    ///
    ///     println!("{:?}", metadata.file_type());
    ///     Ok(())
    /// }
    /// ```
    #[must_use]
    #[stable(feature = "file_type", since = "1.1.0")]
    pub fn file_type(&self) -> FileType {
        FileType(self.0.file_type())
    }

    /// Returns `true` if this metadata is for a directory. The
    /// result is mutually exclusive to the result of
    /// [`Metadata::is_file`], and will be false for symlink metadata
    /// obtained from [`symlink_metadata`].
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```no_run
    /// fn main() -> std::io::Result<()> {
    ///     use std::fs;
    ///
    ///     let metadata = fs::metadata("foo.txt")?;
    ///
    ///     assert!(!metadata.is_dir());
    ///     Ok(())
    /// }
    /// ```
    #[must_use]
    #[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
    pub fn is_dir(&self) -> bool {
        self.file_type().is_dir()
    }

    /// Returns `true` if this metadata is for a regular file. The
    /// result is mutually exclusive to the result of
    /// [`Metadata::is_dir`], and will be false for symlink metadata
    /// obtained from [`symlink_metadata`].
    ///
    /// When the goal is simply to read from (or write to) the source, the most
    /// reliable way to test the source can be read (or written to) is to open
    /// it. Only using `is_file` can break workflows like `diff <( prog_a )` on
    /// a Unix-like system for example. See [`File::open`] or
    /// [`OpenOptions::open`] for more information.
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```no_run
    /// use std::fs;
    ///
    /// fn main() -> std::io::Result<()> {
    ///     let metadata = fs::metadata("foo.txt")?;
    ///

    /// Returns `true` if this metadata is for a symbolic link.

### 737. TLS-liveness precondition (Hooks only runnable while thread-local storage is alive)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/thread/spawnhook.rs:1-155`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: Running spawn hooks implicitly requires that the thread-local `SPAWN_HOOKS` is still alive for the calling thread. When TLS has been destroyed (during thread shutdown/after TLS destructors), `run_spawn_hooks` silently returns an empty/default `ChildSpawnHooks` and skips hook execution. This is enforced by a runtime check (`try_with` failure) rather than by a type-level guarantee that `run_spawn_hooks` cannot be called after TLS teardown.

**Evidence**:

```rust
// Note: Other parts of this module contain: struct SpawnHooks, 1 free function(s), impl Drop for SpawnHooks (1 methods), impl ChildSpawnHooks (1 methods); struct SpawnHook; struct ChildSpawnHooks

use super::thread::Thread;
use crate::cell::Cell;
use crate::iter;
use crate::sync::Arc;

crate::thread_local! {
    /// A thread local linked list of spawn hooks.
    ///
    /// It is a linked list of Arcs, such that it can very cheaply be inherited by spawned threads.
    ///
    /// (That technically makes it a set of linked lists with shared tails, so a linked tree.)
    static SPAWN_HOOKS: Cell<SpawnHooks> = const { Cell::new(SpawnHooks { first: None }) };
}

#[derive(Default, Clone)]
struct SpawnHooks {
    first: Option<Arc<SpawnHook>>,
}

// Manually implement drop to prevent deep recursion when dropping linked Arc list.
impl Drop for SpawnHooks {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        let mut next = self.first.take();
        while let Some(SpawnHook { hook, next: n }) = next.and_then(|n| Arc::into_inner(n)) {
            drop(hook);
            next = n;
        }
    }
}

struct SpawnHook {
    hook: Box<dyn Send + Sync + Fn(&Thread) -> Box<dyn Send + FnOnce()>>,
    next: Option<Arc<SpawnHook>>,
}

/// Registers a function to run for every newly thread spawned.
///
/// The hook is executed in the parent thread, and returns a function
/// that will be executed in the new thread.
///
/// The hook is called with the `Thread` handle for the new thread.
///
/// The hook will only be added for the current thread and is inherited by the threads it spawns.
/// In other words, adding a hook has no effect on already running threads (other than the current
/// thread) and the threads they might spawn in the future.
///
/// Hooks can only be added, not removed.
///
/// The hooks will run in reverse order, starting with the most recently added.
///
/// # Usage
///
/// ```
/// #![feature(thread_spawn_hook)]
///
/// std::thread::add_spawn_hook(|_| {
///     ..; // This will run in the parent (spawning) thread.
///     move || {
///         ..; // This will run it the child (spawned) thread.
///     }
/// });
/// ```
///
/// # Example
///
/// A spawn hook can be used to "inherit" a thread local from the parent thread:
///
/// ```
/// #![feature(thread_spawn_hook)]
///
/// use std::cell::Cell;
///
/// thread_local! {
///     static X: Cell<u32> = Cell::new(0);
/// }
///
/// // This needs to be done once in the main thread before spawning any threads.
/// std::thread::add_spawn_hook(|_| {
///     // Get the value of X in the spawning thread.
///     let value = X.get();
///     // Set the value of X in the newly spawned thread.
///     move || X.set(value)
/// });
///
/// X.set(123);
///
/// std::thread::spawn(|| {
///     assert_eq!(X.get(), 123);
/// }).join().unwrap();
/// ```
#[unstable(feature = "thread_spawn_hook", issue = "132951")]
pub fn add_spawn_hook<F, G>(hook: F)
where
    F: 'static + Send + Sync + Fn(&Thread) -> G,
    G: 'static + Send + FnOnce(),
{
    SPAWN_HOOKS.with(|h| {
        let mut hooks = h.take();
        let next = hooks.first.take();
        hooks.first = Some(Arc::new(SpawnHook {
            hook: Box::new(move |thread| Box::new(hook(thread))),
            next,
        }));
        h.set(hooks);
    });
}

/// Runs all the spawn hooks.
///
/// Called on the parent thread.
///
/// Returns the functions to be called on the newly spawned thread.
pub(super) fn run_spawn_hooks(thread: &Thread) -> ChildSpawnHooks {
    // Get a snapshot of the spawn hooks.
    // (Increments the refcount to the first node.)
    if let Ok(hooks) = SPAWN_HOOKS.try_with(|hooks| {
        let snapshot = hooks.take();
        hooks.set(snapshot.clone());
        snapshot
    }) {
        // Iterate over the hooks, run them, and collect the results in a vector.
        let to_run: Vec<_> = iter::successors(hooks.first.as_deref(), |hook| hook.next.as_deref())
            .map(|hook| (hook.hook)(thread))
            .collect();
        // Pass on the snapshot of the hooks and the results to the new thread,
        // which will then run SpawnHookResults::run().
        ChildSpawnHooks { hooks, to_run }
    } else {
        // TLS has been destroyed. Skip running the hooks.
        // See https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/138696
        ChildSpawnHooks::default()
    }
}

/// The results of running the spawn hooks.
///
/// This struct is sent to the new thread.
/// It contains the inherited hooks and the closures to be run.
#[derive(Default)]
pub(super) struct ChildSpawnHooks {
    hooks: SpawnHooks,
    to_run: Vec<Box<dyn FnOnce() + Send>>,
}

impl ChildSpawnHooks {
    // This is run on the newly spawned thread, directly at the start.
    pub(super) fn run(self) {
        SPAWN_HOOKS.set(self.hooks);
        for run in self.to_run {
            run();
        }
    }
}

```

**Entity:** SPAWN_HOOKS (thread-local Cell<SpawnHooks>) / run_spawn_hooks

**States:** TLS Alive (hooks accessible), TLS Destroyed (hooks inaccessible; skip)

**Transitions:**
- TLS Alive -> TLS Destroyed during thread TLS teardown (outside this module)
- TLS Alive -> (skip) handled by run_spawn_hooks when try_with fails

**Evidence:** `static SPAWN_HOOKS: Cell<SpawnHooks>` thread local definition: hooks are stored in TLS; run_spawn_hooks: `if let Ok(hooks) = SPAWN_HOOKS.try_with(|hooks| { ... }) { ... } else { ... }` branches on TLS availability; run_spawn_hooks else-branch comment: "TLS has been destroyed. Skip running the hooks." and link to rust-lang/rust#138696; else branch returns `ChildSpawnHooks::default()` indicating "no hooks" behavior when precondition is violated

**Implementation:** Thread runtime could pass an explicit capability proving TLS is initialized/alive (e.g., a `TlsAlive` token created before TLS teardown begins) into `run_spawn_hooks(&Thread, &TlsAlive)`. Only code holding `TlsAlive` could request hook execution, preventing calls from TLS-destruction contexts at compile time.

### 962. Standard input FD availability/validity (FD_STDIN must be initialized and open)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/stdio/sgx.rs:1-37`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: All Stdin reads are delegated to `with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDIN, |fd| ...)`, implicitly requiring that the SGX runtime has a valid, open file descriptor bound to `abi::FD_STDIN` at the time of the call. This availability/open-ness is not represented in the `Stdin` type: `Stdin::new()` can be constructed at any time, and `io::Read` methods are always callable, so failures due to missing/invalid/closed stdin can only be detected at runtime (inside `with_std_fd`/`FileDesc` operations).

**Evidence**:

```rust

use crate::sys::fd::FileDesc;

pub struct Stdin;
pub struct Stdout;

// ... (other code) ...

    ret
}

impl Stdin {
    pub const fn new() -> Stdin {
        Stdin
    }
}

impl io::Read for Stdin {
    fn read(&mut self, buf: &mut [u8]) -> io::Result<usize> {
        with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDIN, |fd| fd.read(buf))
    }

    fn read_buf(&mut self, buf: BorrowedCursor<'_>) -> io::Result<()> {
        with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDIN, |fd| fd.read_buf(buf))
    }

    fn read_vectored(&mut self, bufs: &mut [IoSliceMut<'_>]) -> io::Result<usize> {
        with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDIN, |fd| fd.read_vectored(bufs))
    }

    #[inline]
    fn is_read_vectored(&self) -> bool {
        true
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Stdin

**States:** StdinAvailable, StdinUnavailableOrClosed

**Transitions:**
- StdinUnavailableOrClosed -> StdinAvailable via external/runtime initialization of abi::FD_STDIN (not represented in this type/module)
- StdinAvailable -> StdinUnavailableOrClosed via external close/replacement of abi::FD_STDIN (not represented in this type/module)

**Evidence:** pub struct Stdin; (ZST carries no state/capability about FD validity); impl Stdin::new() -> Stdin constructs a value unconditionally (`pub const fn new() -> Stdin { Stdin }`); io::Read::read/read_buf/read_vectored all call `with_std_fd(abi::FD_STDIN, |fd| fd.read*(...))`, implying a required valid mapping from `FD_STDIN` to a usable `FileDesc` at call time; use crate::sys::fd::FileDesc; indicates the underlying operations are performed on an OS/ABI FD that can be invalid/closed at runtime

**Implementation:** Make construction of a readable stdin handle require an explicit capability/token produced by `with_std_fd`/init code, e.g. `struct Stdin(FileDesc)` or `Stdin<'a>{ fd: BorrowedFd<'a> }`, so `io::Read` is only implemented for the validated/borrowed form. `Stdin::new()` could return `Option<Stdin>`/`Result<Stdin>` or be hidden behind an initializer that proves `FD_STDIN` is present.

### 895. DirBuilder path validity precondition (UTF-8 convertible Path required)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/fs/motor.rs:1-17`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: newtype

This is enforced at runtime by converting with Path::to_str() and returning an InvalidFilename error when conversion fails. The type system does not distinguish UTF-8 paths from non-UTF-8 paths, so callers can pass a Path that will always fail on this platform/backend.

### 695. Non-null / non-zero TCS address validity precondition

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/pal/sgx/abi/thread.rs:1-17`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: newtype

**Description**: The API assumes the underlying TCS address for the current thread is never placed at address zero. This is checked at runtime by calling Tcs::new(addr) and aborting if it returns None. The type system does not prevent construction/propagation of an invalid (null/zero) address; instead, validity is enforced by a runtime Option check and an abort message claiming it's a linker error.

**Evidence**:

```rust
use fortanix_sgx_abi::Tcs;

/// Gets the ID for the current thread. The ID is guaranteed to be unique among
/// all currently running threads in the enclave, and it is guaranteed to be
/// constant for the lifetime of the thread. More specifically for SGX, there
/// is a one-to-one correspondence of the ID to the address of the TCS.
#[unstable(feature = "sgx_platform", issue = "56975")]
pub fn current() -> Tcs {
    unsafe extern "C" {
        fn get_tcs_addr() -> *mut u8;
    }
    let addr = unsafe { get_tcs_addr() };
    match Tcs::new(addr) {
        Some(tcs) => tcs,
        None => rtabort!("TCS must not be placed at address zero (this is a linker error)"),
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Tcs (returned by current())

**States:** InvalidTcsAddr (null/zero), ValidTcsAddr (non-zero, forms a Tcs)

**Transitions:**
- InvalidTcsAddr -> (abort) via rtabort! in current()
- ValidTcsAddr -> ValidTcsAddr via successful Tcs::new(addr) in current()

**Evidence:** fn current() -> Tcs: returns a Tcs constructed from a raw address; unsafe extern "C" { fn get_tcs_addr() -> *mut u8; }: raw pointer can be null/zero; match Tcs::new(addr) { Some(tcs) => tcs, None => rtabort!("TCS must not be placed at address zero (this is a linker error)") }: runtime validity gate + invariant in error message

**Implementation:** Introduce a NonZero/NonNull wrapper for the TCS address at the FFI boundary, e.g. have get_tcs_addr return NonNull<u8> (or wrap immediately into a local NonNull<u8> via NonNull::new(addr).expect(...)) and feed that into a constructor that cannot represent zero. If fortanix_sgx_abi::Tcs can be made to store NonNull/NonZero internally, Tcs::new could become infallible for this call site.

### 963. Stdout usage validity depends on external runtime/ABI initialization (Valid / Invalid)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/stdio/solid.rs:1-24`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: Stdout::write() unconditionally calls an unsafe external ABI function (abi::SOLID_LOG_write) and then reports success (Ok(buf.len())) without checking any return value or error condition. This implies an unstated precondition: the underlying SOLID logging facility must be available/initialized and the ABI call must be safe to invoke at this time. The type system does not express whether logging is available, initialized, or permitted; callers can obtain Stdout via const fn new() at any time, even when the backend might not be ready.

**Evidence**:

```rust
// Note: Other parts of this module contain: 2 free function(s)

use crate::sys::pal::abi;

pub type Stdin = unsupported_stdio::Stdin;
pub struct Stdout;
pub type Stderr = Stdout;

impl Stdout {
    pub const fn new() -> Stdout {
        Stdout
    }
}

impl io::Write for Stdout {
    fn write(&mut self, buf: &[u8]) -> io::Result<usize> {
        unsafe { abi::SOLID_LOG_write(buf.as_ptr(), buf.len()) };
        Ok(buf.len())
    }

    fn flush(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> {
        Ok(())
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Stdout

**States:** ValidForLogging, InvalidOrUnavailable

**Transitions:**
- InvalidOrUnavailable -> ValidForLogging via (external/system initialization not represented in this API)

**Evidence:** pub struct Stdout; (ZST with no field encoding readiness/availability state); impl Stdout { pub const fn new() -> Stdout { Stdout } } (can be constructed at any time with no checks); impl io::Write for Stdout::write(): unsafe { abi::SOLID_LOG_write(buf.as_ptr(), buf.len()) }; Ok(buf.len()) (unsafe side-effectful call with assumed preconditions; no error propagation)

**Implementation:** Introduce a token/capability type produced only when the SOLID logging facility is known-initialized (e.g., SolidLogReady). Make Stdout parameterized by or constructed from this capability: Stdout::new(_cap: SolidLogReady) -> Stdout, or Stdout<S> typestate with Stdout<Ready> implementing io::Write. This prevents constructing a writable Stdout when the backend is not available.

### 920. DirEntry path validity / identity invariant (must refer to an existing filesystem entry)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/fs/vexos.rs:1-31`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: DirEntry methods assume its stored `path: PathBuf` continues to identify a real filesystem entry. However, the type is just a owned path, so the entry can be deleted/renamed between creating the DirEntry and calling `metadata()`/`file_type()`, turning it into a stale reference. This is handled via runtime I/O errors, but the type system does not encode whether the DirEntry is backed by a live directory iteration handle, nor does it provide any capability tying the entry to the directory it came from.

    }

    pub fn file_name(&self) -> OsString {
        self.path.file_name().unwrap_or_default().into()
    }

    pub fn metadata(&self) -> io::Result<FileAttr> {
        stat(&self.path)
    }

    pub fn file_type(&self) -> io::Result<FileType> {
        Ok(self.metadata()?.file_type())
    }
}

```

**Entity:** DirEntry

**States:** ValidPathToExistingEntry, StaleOrNonexistentPath

**Transitions:**
- ValidPathToExistingEntry -> StaleOrNonexistentPath via external filesystem mutation (delete/rename/move)

**Evidence:** field `DirEntry { path: PathBuf }` stores only a path (no directory handle / inode / stable identifier); method `metadata(&self) -> io::Result<FileAttr> { stat(&self.path) }` relies on `stat` at call time; failure implies the path is no longer valid; method `file_type(&self) -> io::Result<FileType> { Ok(self.metadata()?.file_type()) }` propagates metadata failure, showing the operation depends on runtime existence

**Implementation:** Represent directory iteration entries as a capability tied to the parent directory handle (e.g., `DirEntry<'a> { dir: &'a DirectoryHandle, name: OsString }`) and perform metadata operations relative to that handle (like `fstatat`-style). This encodes that metadata is queried in the context of the originating directory and can reduce TOCTOU/staleness issues compared to storing only an absolute/standalone `PathBuf`.

### 818. Raw stat layout/ABI compatibility invariant (libc::stat <-> android raw::stat)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/os/android/fs.rs:1-116`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: newtype

**Description**: MetadataExt::as_raw_stat() assumes that the in-memory representation of the platform's `libc::stat` (the actual inner type backing `Metadata`) is layout-compatible with `crate::os::android::raw::stat`. This is enforced only by an unsafe pointer cast, not by the type system. If the two structs ever diverge in size/field order/alignment, calling as_raw_stat() becomes undefined behavior. The safe accessor methods (st_dev, st_ino, ...) avoid this by reading known libc::stat fields directly, but the deprecated as_raw_stat still exposes the unchecked transmute-like view.

**Evidence**:

```rust
#![stable(feature = "metadata_ext", since = "1.1.0")]

use crate::fs::Metadata;
#[allow(deprecated)]
use crate::os::android::raw;
use crate::sys::AsInner;

/// OS-specific extensions to [`fs::Metadata`].
///
/// [`fs::Metadata`]: crate::fs::Metadata
#[stable(feature = "metadata_ext", since = "1.1.0")]
pub trait MetadataExt {
    /// Gain a reference to the underlying `stat` structure which contains
    /// the raw information returned by the OS.
    ///
    /// The contents of the returned `stat` are **not** consistent across
    /// Unix platforms. The `os::unix::fs::MetadataExt` trait contains the
    /// cross-Unix abstractions contained within the raw stat.
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext", since = "1.1.0")]
    #[deprecated(
        since = "1.8.0",
        note = "deprecated in favor of the accessor \
                methods of this trait"
    )]
    #[allow(deprecated)]
    fn as_raw_stat(&self) -> &raw::stat;

    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_dev(&self) -> u64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_ino(&self) -> u64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_mode(&self) -> u32;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_nlink(&self) -> u64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_uid(&self) -> u32;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_gid(&self) -> u32;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_rdev(&self) -> u64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_size(&self) -> u64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_atime(&self) -> i64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_atime_nsec(&self) -> i64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_mtime(&self) -> i64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_mtime_nsec(&self) -> i64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_ctime(&self) -> i64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_ctime_nsec(&self) -> i64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_blksize(&self) -> u64;
    #[stable(feature = "metadata_ext2", since = "1.8.0")]
    fn st_blocks(&self) -> u64;
}

#[stable(feature = "metadata_ext", since = "1.1.0")]
impl MetadataExt for Metadata {
    #[allow(deprecated)]
    fn as_raw_stat(&self) -> &raw::stat {
        unsafe { &*(self.as_inner().as_inner() as *const libc::stat as *const raw::stat) }
    }
    fn st_dev(&self) -> u64 {
        self.as_inner().as_inner().st_dev as u64
    }
    fn st_ino(&self) -> u64 {
        self.as_inner().as_inner().st_ino as u64
    }

    }

        self.as_inner().as_inner().st_nlink as u64
    }
    fn st_uid(&self) -> u32 {
        self.as_inner().as_inner().st_uid as u32
    }
    fn st_gid(&self) -> u32 {
        self.as_inner().as_inner().st_gid as u32
    }
    fn st_rdev(&self) -> u64 {
        self.as_inner().as_inner().st_rdev as u64
    }
    fn st_size(&self) -> u64 {

    }

    }

    }

    }

        self.as_inner().as_inner().st_mtime_nsec as i64
    }

    }

**Entity:** Metadata (via std::os::android::fs::MetadataExt)

**States:** LayoutCompatible, LayoutIncompatible(UB)

**Evidence:** method MetadataExt::as_raw_stat: `unsafe { &*(self.as_inner().as_inner() as *const libc::stat as *const raw::stat) }` performs a raw pointer cast between unrelated struct types; trait docs on as_raw_stat: "Gain a reference to the underlying `stat` structure" and notes platform inconsistency; indicates reliance on OS-provided raw layout; use crate::os::android::raw; and return type `&raw::stat` ties the cast to an Android-specific raw ABI

**Implementation:** Avoid returning `&raw::stat` via casting. Instead, define a newtype wrapper around `libc::stat` (or around the actual inner stat type) and provide a conversion/API that only exposes fields through accessors. If a raw view is still required, gate it behind a trait implemented only when a compile-time layout check passes (e.g., const assertions on size/align and/or `#[repr(C)]` guarantees), or expose a by-value copied representation `raw::stat` constructed field-by-field.

### 419. Child* stream validity precondition (must be a CLOEXEC pipe FD)

### 935. UEFI Stdio capability restrictions (only Inherit/Null/MakePipe are representable)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/process/uefi.rs:1-45`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: On UEFI, only a small subset of stdio configurations are supported. The API surface nonetheless provides `From<io::Stdout>`, `From<io::Stderr>`, and `From<File>` for `Stdio`, but these conversions are not actually valid on this platform and therefore panic at runtime. This encodes an implicit precondition ('do not attempt to construct Stdio from Stdout/Stderr/File on UEFI') that is not enforced by the type system; callers can write code that compiles but deterministically panics on UEFI.

**Evidence**:

```rust
// Note: Other parts of this module contain: struct Command, 2 free function(s), impl Command (15 methods), impl Iterator for CommandArgs < 'a > (2 methods), impl ExactSizeIterator for CommandArgs < 'a > (2 methods); struct ExitStatus, impl ExitStatus (2 methods), impl Into < ExitStatus > for ExitStatusError (1 methods), impl ExitStatusError (1 methods); struct ExitStatusError; struct ExitCode, impl ExitCode (1 methods), impl From < u8 > for ExitCode (1 methods); struct Process, impl Process (4 methods); struct CommandArgs; struct Image, impl Image (15 methods), impl Drop for Image (1 methods); struct PipeProtocol, impl PipeProtocol (14 methods), impl Drop for PipeProtocol (1 methods); struct InputProtocol, impl InputProtocol (4 methods), impl Drop for InputProtocol (1 methods); 3 free function(s)

}

#[derive(Copy, Clone, Debug)]
pub enum Stdio {
    Inherit,
    Null,
    MakePipe,
}

// ... (other code) ...

    Ok((ExitStatus(stat), stdout, stderr))
}

impl From<ChildPipe> for Stdio {
    fn from(pipe: ChildPipe) -> Stdio {
        pipe.diverge()
    }
}

impl From<io::Stdout> for Stdio {
    fn from(_: io::Stdout) -> Stdio {
        // FIXME: This is wrong.
        // Instead, the Stdio we have here should be a unit struct.
        panic!("unsupported")
    }
}

impl From<io::Stderr> for Stdio {
    fn from(_: io::Stderr) -> Stdio {
        // FIXME: This is wrong.
        // Instead, the Stdio we have here should be a unit struct.
        panic!("unsupported")
    }
}

impl From<File> for Stdio {
    fn from(_file: File) -> Stdio {
        // FIXME: This is wrong.
        // Instead, the Stdio we have here should be a unit struct.
        panic!("unsupported")
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Stdio (and its conversions via From<...>)

**States:** Supported(Uefi): Inherit | Null | MakePipe, Unsupported: Stdout | Stderr | File-backed stdio

**Transitions:**
- Stdout -> (panic!) via impl From<io::Stdout> for Stdio
- Stderr -> (panic!) via impl From<io::Stderr> for Stdio
- File -> (panic!) via impl From<File> for Stdio
- ChildPipe -> Supported(Uefi) via impl From<ChildPipe> for Stdio (pipe.diverge())

**Evidence:** pub enum Stdio { Inherit, Null, MakePipe } defines the only representable variants in this module; impl From<io::Stdout> for Stdio: comment `// FIXME: This is wrong.` and `panic!("unsupported")`; impl From<io::Stderr> for Stdio: comment `// FIXME: This is wrong.` and `panic!("unsupported")`; impl From<File> for Stdio: comment `// FIXME: This is wrong.` and `panic!("unsupported")`; impl From<ChildPipe> for Stdio: `pipe.diverge()` indicates a supported conversion path

**Implementation:** Introduce a platform-specific stdio capability type (e.g., `struct UefiStdio(Stdio);` or `enum UefiStdio { Inherit, Null, MakePipe }`) and accept only that in UEFI `Command` APIs. Omit/avoid `From<io::Stdout/Stderr/File>` impls on UEFI (or gate them behind a distinct trait/capability that is not available on UEFI), so unsupported constructions become unrepresentable at compile time instead of panicking.
