pub fn set_times_nofollow(_p: &Path, _times: FileTimes) -> io::Result<()> {
    Err(Error::from_raw_os_error(22))

    run_path_with_cstr(path, &|path| cvt(unsafe { hermit_abi::rmdir(path.as_ptr()) }).map(|_| ()))

pub fn symlink(_original: &Path, _link: &Path) -> io::Result<()> {
    unsupported()

pub fn link(_original: &Path, _link: &Path) -> io::Result<()> {
    unsupported()

pub fn stat(path: &Path) -> io::Result<FileAttr> {
    run_path_with_cstr(path, &|path| {
        let mut stat_val: stat_struct = unsafe { mem::zeroed() };
        cvt(unsafe { hermit_abi::stat(path.as_ptr(), &mut stat_val) })?;
        Ok(FileAttr::from_stat(stat_val))
    })
}

pub f
// ... (truncated) ...
```

**Entity:** OpenOptions

**States:** NoAccessSelected, ReadOnly, WriteOnly, ReadWrite, AppendWriteOnly, AppendReadWrite, InvalidCombination

**Transitions:**
- NoAccessSelected -> {ReadOnly|WriteOnly|ReadWrite|AppendWriteOnly|AppendReadWrite} via read()/write()/append() setters
- AnyAccessState -> InvalidCombination via truncate()/create()/create_new() setters producing forbidden combinations
- ValidCombination -> (flags, mode) via get_access_mode() + get_creation_mode() (used by File::open_c)

**Evidence:** fields: OpenOptions { read: bool, write: bool, append: bool, truncate: bool, create: bool, create_new: bool }; method OpenOptions::get_access_mode(): match (self.read, self.write, self.append) with Err("invalid access mode") when (false,false,false); method OpenOptions::get_creation_mode(): returns Err("invalid creation mode") for disallowed combos (e.g., truncate/create/create_new when neither write nor append; truncate with append unless create_new); File::open_c() relies on opts.get_access_mode()? and opts.get_creation_mode()? to validate at runtime before calling hermit_abi::open

**Implementation:** Replace the booleans with typed states: e.g., OpenOptions<Access, Create>. Access = ReadOnly|WriteOnly|ReadWrite|AppendWriteOnly|AppendReadWrite; Creation = None|Create|Truncate|CreateTruncate|CreateNew. Provide constructors/methods that transition between these states and make invalid transitions unrepresentable. Then File::open can be implemented for OpenOptions<ValidAccess, ValidCreation> only.

### 562. Current-thread handle installation protocol (set_current must match persistent ThreadId and must run before cleanup)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/thread/current.rs:1-332`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: Installing a current-thread handle is a one-time protocol: it must only succeed when CURRENT is uninitialized, and it must not change an already-initialized persistent thread ID. Additionally, successful installation must enable the TLS guard so thread cleanup will call drop_current; otherwise the raw handle would leak or outlive intended cleanup. These constraints are enforced with runtime checks/ordering, not types.

**Evidence**:

```rust
use super::id::ThreadId;
use super::main_thread;
use super::thread::Thread;
use crate::mem::ManuallyDrop;
use crate::ptr;
use crate::sys::thread as imp;
use crate::sys::thread_local::local_pointer;

const NONE: *mut () = ptr::null_mut();
const BUSY: *mut () = ptr::without_provenance_mut(1);
const DESTROYED: *mut () = ptr::without_provenance_mut(2);

local_pointer! {
    static CURRENT;
}

/// Persistent storage for the thread ID.
///
/// We store the thread ID so that it never gets destroyed during the lifetime
/// of a thread, either using `#[thread_local]` or multiple `local_pointer!`s.
pub(super) mod id {
    use super::*;

    cfg_select! {
        target_thread_local => {
            use crate::cell::Cell;

            #[thread_local]
            static ID: Cell<Option<ThreadId>> = Cell::new(None);

            pub(super) const CHEAP: bool = true;

            pub(crate) fn get() -> Option<ThreadId> {
                ID.get()
            }

            pub(super) fn set(id: ThreadId) {
                ID.set(Some(id))
            }
        }
        target_pointer_width = "16" => {
            local_pointer! {
                static ID0;
                static ID16;
                static ID32;
                static ID48;
            }

            pub(super) const CHEAP: bool = false;

            pub(crate) fn get() -> Option<ThreadId> {
                let id0 = ID0.get().addr() as u64;
                let id16 = ID16.get().addr() as u64;
                let id32 = ID32.get().addr() as u64;
                let id48 = ID48.get().addr() as u64;
                ThreadId::from_u64((id48 << 48) + (id32 << 32) + (id16 << 16) + id0)
            }

            pub(super) fn set(id: ThreadId) {
                let val = id.as_u64().get();
                ID0.set(ptr::without_provenance_mut(val as usize));
                ID16.set(ptr::without_provenance_mut((val >> 16) as usize));
                ID32.set(ptr::without_provenance_mut((val >> 32) as usize));
                ID48.set(ptr::without_provenance_mut((val >> 48) as usize));
            }
        }
        target_pointer_width = "32" => {
            local_pointer! {
                static ID0;
                static ID32;
            }

            pub(super) const CHEAP: bool = false;

            pub(crate) fn get() -> Option<ThreadId> {
                let id0 = ID0.get().addr() as u64;
                let id32 = ID32.get().addr() as u64;
                ThreadId::from_u64((id32 << 32) + id0)
            }

            pub(super) fn set(id: ThreadId) {
                let val = id.as_u64().get();
                ID0.set(ptr::without_provenance_mut(val as usize));
                ID32.set(ptr::without_provenance_mut((val >> 32) as usize));
            }
        }
        _ => {
            local_pointer! {
                static ID;
            }

            pub(super) const CHEAP: bool = true;

            pub(crate) fn get() -> Option<ThreadId> {
                let id = ID.get().addr() as u64;
                ThreadId::from_u64(id)
            }

            pub(super) fn set(id: ThreadId) {
                let val = id.as_u64().get();
                ID.set(ptr::without_provenance_mut(val as usize));
            }
        }
    }

    #[inline]
    pub(super) fn get_or_init() -> ThreadId {
        get().unwrap_or_else(
            #[cold]
            || {
                let id = ThreadId::new();
                id::set(id);
                id
            },
        )
    }
}

/// Tries to set the thread handle for the current thread. Fails if a handle was
/// already set or if the thread ID of `thread` would change an already-set ID.
pub(super) fn set_current(thread: Thread) -> Result<(), Thread> {
    if CURRENT.get() != NONE {
        return Err(thread);
    }

    match id::get() {
        Some(id) if id == thread.id() => {}
        None => id::set(thread.id()),
        _ => return Err(thread),
    }

    // Make sure that `crate::rt::thread_cleanup` will be run, which will
    // call `drop_current`.
    crate::sys::thread_local::guard::enable();
    CURRENT.set(thread.into_raw().cast_mut());
    Ok(())
}

/// Gets the unique identifier of the thread which invokes it.
///
/// Calling this function may be more efficient than accessing the current
/// thread id through the current thread handle. i.e. `thread::current().id()`.
///
/// This function will always succeed, will always return the same value for
/// one thread and is guaranteed not to call the global allocator.
///
/// # Examples
///
/// ```
/// #![feature(current_thread_id)]
///
/// use std::thread;
///
/// let other_thread = thread::spawn(|| {
///     thread::current_id()
/// });
///
/// let other_thread_id = other_thread.join().unwrap();
/// assert_ne!(thread::current_id(), other_thread_id);
/// ```
#[inline]
#[must_use]
#[unstable(feature = "current_thread_id", issue = "147194")]
pub fn current_id() -> ThreadId {
    // If accessing the persistent thread ID takes multiple TLS accesses, try
    // to retrieve it from the current thread handle, which will only take one
    // TLS access.
    if !id::CHEAP {
        if let Some(id) = try_with_current(|t| t.map(|t| t.id())) {
            return id;
        }
    }

    id::get_or_init()
}

/// Gets the OS thread ID of the thread that invokes it, if available. If not, return the Rust
/// thread ID.
///
/// We use a `u64` to all possible platform IDs without excess `cfg`; most use `int`, some use a
/// pointer, and Apple uses `uint64_t`. This is a "best effort" approach for diagnostics and is
/// allowed to fall back to a non-OS ID (such as the Rust thread ID) or a non-unique ID (such as a
/// PID) if the thread ID cannot be retrieved.
pub(crate) fn current_os_id() -> u64 {
    imp::current_os_id().unwrap_or_else(|| current_id().as_u64().get())
}

/// Gets a reference to the handle of the thread that invokes it, if the handle
/// has been initialized.
fn try_with_current<F, R>(f: F) -> R
where
    F: FnOnce(Option<&Thread>) -> R,
{
    let current = CURRENT.get();
    if current > DESTROYED {
        // SAFETY: `Arc` does not contain interior mutability, so it does not
        // matter that the address of the handle might be different depending
        // on where this is called.
        unsafe {
            let current = ManuallyDrop::new(Thread::from_raw(current));
            f(Some(&current))
        }
    } else {
        f(None)
    }
}

/// Run a function with the current thread's name.
///
/// Modulo thread local accesses, this function is safe to call from signal
/// handlers and in similar circumstances where allocations are not possible.
pub(crate) fn with_current_name<F, R>(f: F) -> R
where
    F: FnOnce(Option<&str>) -> R,
{
    try_with_current(|thread| {
        let name = if let Some(thread) = thread {
            // If there is a current thread handle, try to use the name stored
            // there.
            thread.name()
        } else if let Some(main) = main_thread::get()
            && let Some(id) = id::get()
            && id == main
        {
            // The main thread doesn't always have a thread handle, we must
            // identify it through its ID instead. The checks are ordered so
            // that the current ID is only loaded if it is actually needed,
            // since loading it from TLS might need multiple expensive accesses.
            Some("main")
        } else {
            None
        };

        f(name)
    })
}

/// Gets a handle to the thread that invokes it. If the handle stored in thread-
/// local storage was already destroyed, this creates a new unnamed temporary
/// handle to allow thread parking in nearly all situations.
pub(crate) fn current_or_unnamed() -> Thread {
    let current = CURRENT.get();
    if current > DESTROYED {
        unsafe {
            let current = ManuallyDrop::new(Thread::from_raw(current));
            (*current).clone()
        }
    } else if current == DESTROYED {
        Thread::new(id::get_or_init(), None)
    } else {
        init_current(current)
    }
}

/// Gets a handle to the thread that invokes it.
///
/// # Examples
///
/// Getting a handle to the current thread with `thread::current()`:
///
/// ```
/// use std::thread;
///
/// let handler = thread::Builder::new()
///     .name("named thread".into())
///     .spawn(|| {
///         let handle = thread::current();
///         assert_eq!(handle.name(), Some("named thread"));
///     })
///     .unwrap();
///
/// handler.join().unwrap();
/// ```
#[must_use]
#[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
pub fn current() -> Thread {
    let current = CURRENT.get();
    if current > DESTROYED {
        unsafe {
            let current = ManuallyDrop::new(Thread::from_raw(current));
            (*current).clone()
        }
    } else {
        init_current(current)
    }
}

#[cold]
fn init_current(current: *mut ()) -> Thread {
    if current == NONE {
        CURRENT.set(BUSY);
        // If the thread ID was initialized already, use it.
        let id = id::get_or_init();
        let thread = Thread::new(id, None);

        // Make sure that `crate::rt::thread_cleanup` will be run, which will
        // call `drop_current`.
        crate::sys::thread_local::guard::enable();
        CURRENT.set(thread.clone().into_raw().cast_mut());
        thread
    } else if current == BUSY {
        // BUSY exists solely for this check, but as it is in the slow path, the
        // extra TLS write above shouldn't matter. The alternative is nearly always
        // a stack overflow.
        //
        // If we reach this point it means our initialization routine ended up
        // calling current() either directly, or indirectly through the global
        // allocator, which is a bug either way as we may not call the global
        // allocator in current().
        rtabort!(
            "init_current() was re-entrant, which indicates a bug in the Rust threading implementation"
        )
    } else {
        debug_assert_eq!(current, DESTROYED);
        panic!(
            "use of std::thread::current() is not possible after the thread's \
            local data has been destroyed"
        )
    }
}

/// This should be run in [`crate::rt::thread_cleanup`] to reset the thread
/// handle.
pub(crate) fn drop_current() {
    let current = CURRENT.get();
    if current > DESTROYED {
        unsafe {
            CURRENT.set(DESTROYED);
            drop(Thread::from_raw(current));
        }
    }
}

```

**Entity:** set_current(thread: Thread) / interaction between CURRENT and id::ID

**States:** No handle installed, Handle installed with matching persistent ThreadId, Rejected (already installed or ID mismatch)

**Transitions:**
- No handle installed -> Handle installed via set_current(thread) when CURRENT==NONE and ThreadId is unset or matches
- No handle installed/Handle installed -> Rejected via set_current(thread) returning Err(thread) when CURRENT!=NONE or when id::get() mismatches
- Handle installed -> (cleanup scheduled) via guard::enable() ordering before CURRENT.set(raw)

**Evidence:** set_current(): `if CURRENT.get() != NONE { return Err(thread); }` (one-time install / already-installed invariant); set_current(): `match id::get() { Some(id) if id == thread.id() => {}, None => id::set(thread.id()), _ => return Err(thread) }` (persistent ID must be consistent; cannot change once set); set_current(): comment and call ordering: `// Make sure that crate::rt::thread_cleanup will be run, which will call drop_current.` then `guard::enable(); CURRENT.set(thread.into_raw()...)` (temporal ordering requirement)

**Implementation:** Introduce an internal, non-constructible capability/token representing “may install current thread handle”, produced only by the runtime during thread startup (e.g., `struct SetCurrentToken(Private);`). Change `set_current(thread, token)` so only the runtime path can call it, and couple it to a constructor that also enables the guard (or returns a RAII guard that on Drop performs `drop_current`). This prevents accidental double-install and enforces ordering (`guard::enable` before publishing pointer) inside the safe API boundary.

### 358. Waiter memory/notification protocol (signaled-before-free; next/thread accessed before signaling)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/sync/once/queue.rs:1-42`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: session_type

**Description**: A Waiter node participates in a concurrent notification protocol: once `signaled` is set to true, the waiting thread may observe it and free the Waiter node (e.g., due to spurious wakeups). Therefore, code that signals a node must first copy out any needed fields (`thread`, `next`) while the node is still guaranteed valid, then publish `signaled=true` with Release ordering, then unpark. These ordering and lifetime requirements are enforced only by comments and `unsafe` pointer traversal; the type system does not prevent use-after-free if a future change reorders operations (e.g., reading `next` after setting `signaled`).

**Evidence**:

```rust
// Note: Other parts of this module contain: struct Once, impl Once (6 methods), impl OnceState (2 methods); struct OnceState; struct WaiterQueue; 2 free function(s)

// a shared reference to check `signaled`. Instead we hold shared references and
// use interior mutability.
#[repr(align(4))] // Ensure the two lower bits are free to use as state bits.
struct Waiter {
    thread: Thread,
    signaled: Atomic<bool>,
    next: Cell<*const Waiter>,
}

// ... (other code) ...

    }
}

impl Drop for WaiterQueue<'_> {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        // Swap out our state with however we finished.
        let current = self.state_and_queue.swap(self.set_state_on_drop_to, AcqRel);

        // We should only ever see an old state which was RUNNING.
        assert_eq!(current.addr() & STATE_MASK, RUNNING);

        // Walk the entire linked list of waiters and wake them up (in lifo
        // order, last to register is first to wake up).
        unsafe {
            // Right after setting `node.signaled = true` the other thread may
            // free `node` if there happens to be has a spurious wakeup.
            // So we have to take out the `thread` field and copy the pointer to
            // `next` first.
            let mut queue = to_queue(current);
            while !queue.is_null() {
                let next = (*queue).next.get();
                let thread = (*queue).thread.clone();
                (*queue).signaled.store(true, Release);
                thread.unpark();
                queue = next;
            }
        }
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Waiter

**States:** RegisteredInQueue, SignaledAndMayBeFreed

**Transitions:**
- RegisteredInQueue -> SignaledAndMayBeFreed via `signaled.store(true, Release)` in WaiterQueue::drop

**Evidence:** Waiter fields: `signaled: Atomic<bool>`, `next: Cell<*const Waiter>`, `thread: Thread` (encodes the protocol data); Drop for WaiterQueue<'_>: comment: "Right after setting `node.signaled = true` the other thread may free `node` ... So we have to take out the `thread` field and copy the pointer to `next` first." (explicit ordering/lifetime invariant); Drop for WaiterQueue<'_>: `let next = (*queue).next.get(); let thread = (*queue).thread.clone(); (*queue).signaled.store(true, Release); thread.unpark();` (relies on this specific order to avoid UAF); Drop for WaiterQueue<'_>: `unsafe { ... while !queue.is_null() { ... } }` (raw-pointer traversal depends on protocol correctness)

**Implementation:** Encapsulate the signal sequence in a safe API that enforces the step ordering, e.g. a `WaiterRef<'a>` wrapper created from the raw pointer that exposes `fn prepare_to_signal(&self) -> (Thread, *const Waiter)` (reads/copies `thread`+`next`) and then `fn signal(self)` (does the Release store). By consuming a token returned from `prepare_to_signal`, you make it impossible to call `signal` before extracting required fields, and keep the raw-pointer deref localized to one module.

---

### 351. PanicGuard lifetime protocol for intrusive-list nodes (Must not drop)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/sync/rwlock/queue.rs:1-12`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: PanicGuard is used as a sentinel to prevent unwinding past a point where intrusive-list nodes are expected to be stack-allocated and must not be dropped. The implicit protocol is that this guard must never reach its Drop in normal execution; if it does, the code aborts, indicating a violated invariant (e.g., a stack unwind tried to drop a node/guard while the node is linked). This is enforced only at runtime via an abort in Drop, rather than being encoded in types/lifetimes (e.g., by making the relevant operations panic-unwind-safe or by tying node lifetimes to a scope token that cannot be dropped while linked).

**Evidence**:

```rust
// Note: Other parts of this module contain: struct Node, 3 free function(s), impl Node (4 methods); struct AtomicLink, impl AtomicLink (3 methods); struct RwLock, impl RwLock (13 methods); 2 free function(s)

}

/// A type to guard against the unwinds of stacks that nodes are located on due to panics.
struct PanicGuard;

impl Drop for PanicGuard {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        rtabort!("tried to drop node in intrusive list.");
    }
}

```

**Entity:** PanicGuard

**States:** Active (in-scope), Dropped (should be unreachable)

**Transitions:**
- Active (in-scope) -> Dropped via scope exit/unwind triggering Drop

**Evidence:** comment: "A type to guard against the unwinds of stacks that nodes are located on due to panics." (implies an unwind/temporal protocol); impl Drop for PanicGuard: drop() calls `rtabort!("tried to drop node in intrusive list.")` (runtime enforcement that Drop must not run)

**Implementation:** Introduce a non-droppable/consume-only scope token (e.g., `struct NoUnwindScope<'a>(PhantomData<&'a mut ()>);`) created by an API that requires `catch_unwind` around the critical section, or require callers to pass a capability proving they are in a `no_unwind` region. Alternatively, model the intrusive-list membership with a typestate `Node<Linked>`/`Node<Unlinked>` and only allow linking when a scope token is held, preventing operations that would allow unwinding/dropping while linked.

---

### 289. UdpSocket I/O mode and timeout coupling (Blocking/Nonblocking changes timeout semantics)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/net/connection/xous/udp.rs:1-436`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: typestate

**Description**: The socket has a runtime I/O mode (`nonblocking: Cell<bool>`) that changes the meaning/effect of `read_timeout`/`write_timeout`. For recv: in nonblocking mode, timeout bytes are not sent and the operation is 'would block' style; in blocking mode, `read_timeout` is serialized into the request. For send: in nonblocking mode the effective write timeout is forced to ZERO; in blocking mode `write_timeout==0` means 'forever' (u64::MAX) otherwise a finite duration. These coupled semantics are not expressed in types, so code can set timeouts expecting them to apply while in nonblocking mode (they won’t), and correctness depends on call ordering and mode assumptions.

**Evidence**:

```rust
}

#[derive(Clone)]
pub struct UdpSocket {
    fd: u16,
    local: SocketAddr,
    remote: Cell<Option<SocketAddr>>,
    // in milliseconds. The setting applies only to `recv` calls after the timeout is set.
    read_timeout: Cell<u64>,
    // in milliseconds. The setting applies only to `send` calls after the timeout is set.
    write_timeout: Cell<u64>,
    handle_count: Arc<Atomic<usize>>,
    nonblocking: Cell<bool>,
}

impl UdpSocket {
    pub fn bind<A: ToSocketAddrs>(addr: A) -> io::Result<UdpSocket> {
        return each_addr(addr, inner);

        fn inner(addr: &SocketAddr) -> io::Result<UdpSocket> {
            // Construct the request
            let mut connect_request = ConnectRequest { raw: [0u8; 4096] };

            // Serialize the StdUdpBind structure. This is done "manually" because we don't want to
            // make an auto-serdes (like bincode or rkyv) crate a dependency of Xous.
            let port_bytes = addr.port().to_le_bytes();
            connect_request.raw[0] = port_bytes[0];
            connect_request.raw[1] = port_bytes[1];
            match addr.ip() {
                IpAddr::V4(addr) => {
                    connect_request.raw[2] = 4;
                    for (dest, src) in connect_request.raw[3..].iter_mut().zip(addr.octets()) {
                        *dest = src;
                    }
                }
                IpAddr::V6(addr) => {
                    connect_request.raw[2] = 6;
                    for (dest, src) in connect_request.raw[3..].iter_mut().zip(addr.octets()) {
                        *dest = src;
                    }
                }
            }

            let response = crate::os::xous::ffi::lend_mut(
                services::net_server(),
                services::NetLendMut::StdUdpBind.into(),
                &mut connect_request.raw,
                0,
                4096,
            );

            let Ok((_, valid)) = response else {
                return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput, "invalid response"));
            };

            // The first four bytes should be zero upon success, and will be nonzero
            // for an error.
            let response = connect_request.raw;
            if response[0] != 0 || valid == 0 {
                let errcode = response[1];
                if errcode == NetError::SocketInUse as u8 {
                    return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::ResourceBusy, "socket in use"));
                } else if errcode == NetError::Invalid as u8 {
                    return Err(io::const_error!(
                        io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput,
                        "port can't be 0 or invalid address",
                    ));
                } else if errcode == NetError::LibraryError as u8 {
                    return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::Other, "library error"));
                } else {
                    return Err(io::const_error!(
                        io::ErrorKind::Other,
                        "unable to connect or internal error",
                    ));
                }
            }

            let fd = response[1] as u16;
            Ok(UdpSocket {
                fd,
                local: *addr,
                remote: Cell::new(None),
                read_timeout: Cell::new(0),
                write_timeout: Cell::new(0),
                handle_count: Arc::new(AtomicUsize::new(1)),
                nonblocking: Cell::new(false),
            })
        }
    }

    pub fn peer_addr(&self) -> io::Result<SocketAddr> {
        match self.remote.get() {
            Some(dest) => Ok(dest),
            None => Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::NotConnected, "no peer specified")),
        }
    }

    pub fn socket_addr(&self) -> io::Result<SocketAddr> {
        Ok(self.local)
    }

    fn recv_inner(&self, buf: &mut [u8], do_peek: bool) -> io::Result<(usize, SocketAddr)> {
        let mut receive_request = ReceiveData { raw: [0u8; 4096] };

        if self.nonblocking.get() {
            // nonblocking
            receive_request.raw[0] = 0;
        } else {
            // blocking
            receive_request.raw[0] = 1;
            for (&s, d) in self
                .read_timeout
                .get()
                .to_le_bytes()
                .iter()
                .zip(receive_request.raw[1..9].iter_mut())
            {
                *d = s;
            }
        }
        if let Ok((_offset, _valid)) = crate::os::xous::ffi::lend_mut(
            services::net_server(),
            services::NetLendMut::StdUdpRx(self.fd).into(),
            &mut receive_request.raw,
            if do_peek { 1 } else { 0 },
            0,
        ) {
            if receive_request.raw[0] != 0 {
                // error case
                if receive_request.raw[1] == NetError::TimedOut as u8 {
                    return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::TimedOut, "recv timed out"));
                } else if receive_request.raw[1] == NetError::WouldBlock as u8 {
                    return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::WouldBlock, "recv would block"));
                } else if receive_request.raw[1] == NetError::LibraryError as u8 {
                    return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::Other, "library error"));
                } else {
                    return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::Other, "library error"));
                }
            } else {
                let rr = &receive_request.raw;
                let rxlen = u16::from_le_bytes(rr[1..3].try_into().unwrap());
                let port = u16::from_le_bytes(rr[20..22].try_into().unwrap());
                let addr = if rr[3] == 4 {
                    SocketAddr::new(IpAddr::V4(Ipv4Addr::new(rr[4], rr[5], rr[6], rr[7])), port)
                } else if rr[3] == 6 {
                    SocketAddr::new(
                        IpAddr::V6(Ipv6Addr::new(
                            u16::from_be_bytes(rr[4..6].try_into().unwrap()),
                            u16::from_be_bytes(rr[6..8].try_into().unwrap()),
                            u16::from_be_bytes(rr[8..10].try_into().unwrap()),
                            u16::from_be_bytes(rr[10..12].try_into().unwrap()),
                            u16::from_be_bytes(rr[12..14].try_into().unwrap()),
                            u16::from_be_bytes(rr[14..16].try_into().unwrap()),
                            u16::from_be_bytes(rr[16..18].try_into().unwrap()),
                            u16::from_be_bytes(rr[18..20].try_into().unwrap()),
                        )),
                        port,
                    )
                } else {
                    return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::Other, "library error"));
                };
                for (&s, d) in rr[22..22 + rxlen as usize].iter().zip(buf.iter_mut()) {
                    *d = s;
                }
                Ok((rxlen as usize, addr))
            }
        } else {
            Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput, "unable to recv"))
        }
    }

    pub fn recv_from(&self, buf: &mut [u8]) -> io::Result<(usize, SocketAddr)> {
        self.recv_inner(buf, false)
    }

    pub fn recv(&self, buf: &mut [u8]) -> io::Result<usize> {
        self.recv_from(buf).map(|(len, _addr)| len)
    }

    pub fn peek_from(&self, buf: &mut [u8]) -> io::Result<(usize, SocketAddr)> {
        self.recv_inner(buf, true)
    }

    pub fn peek(&self, buf: &mut [u8]) -> io::Result<usize> {
        self.peek_from(buf).map(|(len, _addr)| len)
    }

    pub fn connect<A: ToSocketAddrs>(&self, addr: A) -> io::Result<()> {
        each_addr(addr, |addr| {
            self.remote.set(Some(*addr));
            Ok(())
        })
    }

    pub fn send(&self, buf: &[u8]) -> io::Result<usize> {
        if let Some(addr) = self.remote.get() {
            self.send_to(buf, &addr)
        } else {
            Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::NotConnected, "No remote specified"))
        }
    }

    pub fn send_to(&self, buf: &[u8], addr: &SocketAddr) -> io::Result<usize> {
        let mut tx_req = SendData { raw: [0u8; 4096] };

        // Construct the request.
        let port_bytes = addr.port().to_le_bytes();
        tx_req.raw[0] = port_bytes[0];
        tx_req.raw[1] = port_bytes[1];
        match addr.ip() {
            IpAddr::V4(addr) => {
                tx_req.raw[2] = 4;
                for (dest, src) in tx_req.raw[3..].iter_mut().zip(addr.octets()) {
                    *dest = src;
                }
            }
            IpAddr::V6(addr) => {
                tx_req.raw[2] = 6;
                for (dest, src) in tx_req.raw[3..].iter_mut().zip(addr.octets()) {
                    *dest = src;
                }
            }
        }
        let len = buf.len() as u16;
        let len_bytes = len.to_le_bytes();
        tx_req.raw[19] = len_bytes[0];
        tx_req.raw[20] = len_bytes[1];
        for (&s, d) in buf.iter().zip(tx_req.raw[21..].iter_mut()) {
            *d = s;
        }

        // let buf = unsafe {
        //     xous::MemoryRange::new(
        //         &mut tx_req as *mut SendData as usize,
        //         size_of::<SendData>(),
        //     )
        //     .unwrap()
        // };

        // write time-outs are implemented on the caller side. Basically, if the Net crate server
        // is too busy to take the call immediately: retry, until the timeout is reached.
        let now = crate::time::Instant::now();
        let write_timeout = if self.nonblocking.get() {
            // nonblocking
            core::time::Duration::ZERO
        } else {
            // blocking
            if self.write_timeout.get() == 0 {
                // forever
                core::time::Duration::from_millis(u64::MAX)
            } else {
                // or this amount of time
                core::time::Duration::from_millis(self.write_timeout.get())
            }
        };
        loop {
            let response = crate::os::xous::ffi::try_lend_mut(
                services::net_server(),
                services::NetLendMut::StdUdpTx(self.fd).into(),
                &mut tx_req.raw,
                0,
                4096,
            );
            match response {
                Ok((_, valid)) => {
                    let response = &tx_req.raw;
                    if response[0] != 0 || valid == 0 {
                        let errcode = response[1];
                        if errcode == NetError::SocketInUse as u8 {
                            return Err(io::const_error!(
                                io::ErrorKind::ResourceBusy,
                                "socket in use",
                            ));
                        } else if errcode == NetError::Invalid as u8 {
                            return Err(io::const_error!(
                                io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput,
                                "socket not valid",
                            ));
                        } else if errcode == NetError::LibraryError as u8 {
                            return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::Other, "library error"));
                        } else {
                            return Err(io::const_error!(
                                io::ErrorKind::Other,
                                "unable to connect",
                            ));
                        }
                    } else {
                        // no error
                        return Ok(len as usize);
                    }
                }
                Err(crate::os::xous::ffi::Error::ServerQueueFull) => {
                    if now.elapsed() >= write_timeout {
                        return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::WouldBlock, "write timed out"));
                    } else {
                        // question: do we want to do something a bit more gentle than immediately retrying?
                        crate::thread::yield_now();
                    }
                }
                _ => return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::Other, "library error")),
            }
        }
    }

    pub fn duplicate(&self) -> io::Result<UdpSocket> {
        self.handle_count.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
        Ok(self.clone())
    }

    pub fn set_read_timeout(&self, timeout: Option<Duration>) -> io::Result<()> {
        if let Some(d) = timeout {
            if d.is_zero() {
                return Err(io::Error::ZERO_TIMEOUT);
            }
        }
        self.read_timeout
            .set(timeout.map(|t| t.as_millis().min(u64::MAX as u128) as u64).unwrap_or_default());
        Ok(())
    }

    pub fn set_write_timeout(&self, timeout: Option<Duration>) -> io::Result<()> {
        if let Some(d) = timeout {
            if d.is_zero() {
                return Err(io::Error::ZERO_TIMEOUT);
            }
        }
        self.write_timeout
            .set(timeout.map(|t| t.as_millis().min(u64::MAX as u128) as u64).unwrap_or_default());
        Ok(())
    }

    pub fn read_timeout(&self) -> io::Result<Option<Duration>> {
        match self.read_timeout.get() {
            0 => Ok(None),
            t => Ok(Some(Duration::from_millis(t as u64))),
        }
    }

    pub fn write_timeout(&self) -> io::Result<Option<Duration>> {
        match self.write_timeout.get() {
            0 => Ok(None),
            t => Ok(Some(Duration::from_millis(t as u64))),
        }
    }

    pub fn set_ttl(&self, ttl: u32) -> io::Result<()> {
        if ttl > 255 {
            return Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput, "TTL must be less than 256"));
        }
        crate::os::xous::ffi::blocking_scalar(
            services::net_server(),
            services::NetBlockingScalar::StdSetTtlUdp(self.fd, ttl).into(),
        )
        .or(Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput, "unexpected return value")))
        .map(|_| ())
    }

    pub fn ttl(&self) -> io::Result<u32> {
        Ok(crate::os::xous::ffi::blocking_scalar(
            services::net_server(),
            services::NetBlockingScalar::StdGetTtlUdp(self.fd).into(),
        )
        .or(Err(io::const_error!(io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput, "unexpected return value")))
        .map(|res| res[0] as _)?)
    }

    pub fn take_error(&self) -> io::Result<Option<io::Error>> {
        // this call doesn't have a meaning on our platform, but we can at least not panic if it's used.
        Ok(None)
    }

    pub fn set_nonblocking(&self, nonblocking: bool) -> io::Result<()> {
        self.nonblocking.set(nonblocking);
        Ok(())
    }

    // ------------- smoltcp base stack does not have multicast or broadcast support ---------------
    pub fn set_broadcast(&self, _: bool) -> io::Result<()> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn broadcast(&self) -> io::Result<bool> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn set_multicast_loop_v4(&self, _: bool) -> io::Result<()> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn multicast_loop_v4(&self) -> io::Result<bool> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn set_multicast_ttl_v4(&self, _: u32) -> io::Result<()> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn multicast_ttl_v4(&self) -> io::Result<u32> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn set_multicast_loop_v6(&self, _: bool) -> io::Result<()> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn multicast_loop_v6(&self) -> io::Result<bool> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn join_multicast_v4(&self, _: &Ipv4Addr, _: &Ipv4Addr) -> io::Result<()> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn join_multicast_v6(&self, _: &Ipv6Addr, _: u32) -> io::Result<()> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn leave_multicast_v4(&self, _: &Ipv4Addr, _: &Ipv4Addr) -> io::Result<()> {
        unimpl!();
    }

    pub fn leave_multicast_v6(&self, _: &Ipv6Addr, _: u32) -> io::Result<()> {
        unimpl!();
    }
}

// ... (other code) ...

    }
}

impl Drop for UdpSocket {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if self.handle_count.fetch_sub(1, Ordering::Relaxed) == 1 {
           
// ... (truncated) ...
```

**Entity:** UdpSocket

**States:** Blocking mode, Nonblocking mode

**Transitions:**
- Blocking -> Nonblocking via set_nonblocking(true)
- Nonblocking -> Blocking via set_nonblocking(false)

**Evidence:** `nonblocking: Cell<bool>` field tracks mode at runtime; set_nonblocking(): `self.nonblocking.set(nonblocking);`; recv_inner(): `if self.nonblocking.get() { receive_request.raw[0] = 0; } else { receive_request.raw[0] = 1; ... serialize self.read_timeout ... }`; comment on read_timeout: `The setting applies only to recv calls after the timeout is set.` (temporal/semantic coupling); send_to(): `let write_timeout = if self.nonblocking.get() { Duration::ZERO } else { ... if self.write_timeout.get() == 0 { Duration::from_millis(u64::MAX) } ... }`; send_to(): retries on `Error::ServerQueueFull` until `now.elapsed() >= write_timeout`, returning `WouldBlock` with message `"write timed out"`

**Implementation:** Split into `UdpSocket<Blocking>` and `UdpSocket<Nonblocking>` (or `BlockingUdpSocket`/`NonblockingUdpSocket` newtypes). Make `set_nonblocking(self, ...) -> UdpSocket<...>` consume and return the new mode. Optionally restrict/rename timeout setters so their semantics are explicit per mode (e.g., `set_read_timeout_blocking` only on Blocking).

### 763. Receiver draining protocol via Iter (Open/Disconnected/Empty semantics surfaced as Option)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sync/mpsc.rs:1-42`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: typestate

**Description**: Iter<'a, T> is an adapter that repeatedly calls Receiver::recv() and maps its Result to Option via ok(). This implicitly relies on the Receiver protocol: iteration yields items while the channel can produce values, and terminates permanently once recv() returns Err (typically when all senders are dropped and the channel is empty). The type system does not express the distinction between 'temporarily no value yet (blocking)' and 'permanently no more values (disconnected)', nor does it prevent using Iter in contexts where blocking is undesirable; these semantics are encoded by recv() + ok() at runtime.

**Evidence**:

```rust
// Note: Other parts of this module contain: struct Receiver, 2 free function(s), impl Send for Receiver < T > (0 methods), impl Sync for Receiver < T > (0 methods), impl Receiver < T > (6 methods), impl IntoIterator for & 'a Receiver < T > (1 methods), impl IntoIterator for Receiver < T > (1 methods); struct TryIter; struct IntoIter; struct Sender, impl Send for Sender < T > (0 methods), impl Sync for Sender < T > (0 methods), impl Send for SyncSender < T > (0 methods), impl Sender < T > (1 methods), impl SyncSender < T > (3 methods); struct SyncSender; struct SendError, impl error :: Error for SendError < T > (0 methods), impl error :: Error for TrySendError < T > (0 methods), impl From < SendError < T > > for TrySendError < T > (1 methods); struct RecvError, impl error :: Error for RecvError (0 methods), impl error :: Error for TryRecvError (0 methods), impl From < RecvError > for TryRecvError (1 methods); enum TryRecvError; enum RecvTimeoutError, impl error :: Error for RecvTimeoutError (0 methods), impl From < RecvError > for RecvTimeoutError (1 methods); enum TrySendError

/// ```
#[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Iter<'a, T: 'a> {
    rx: &'a Receiver<T>,
}

// ... (other code) ...

}

#[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
impl<'a, T> Iterator for Iter<'a, T> {
    type Item = T;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<T> {
        self.rx.recv().ok()
    }
}

#[stable(feature = "receiver_try_iter", since = "1.15.0")]
impl<'a, T> Iterator for TryIter<'a, T> {
    type Item = T;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<T> {
        self.rx.try_recv().ok()
    }
}

// ... (other code) ...

}

#[stable(feature = "receiver_into_iter", since = "1.1.0")]
impl<T> Iterator for IntoIter<T> {
    type Item = T;
    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<T> {
        self.rx.recv().ok()
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Iter<'a, T>

**States:** Receiving (connected or may block), Terminated (disconnected/EOF)

**Transitions:**
- Receiving -> Terminated via Receiver::recv() returning Err, mapped to None by ok()

**Evidence:** struct Iter { rx: &'a Receiver<T> } stores a shared Receiver reference and delegates to it; impl Iterator for Iter::next: `self.rx.recv().ok()` converts `Result<T, RecvError>` into `Option<T>` (Err becomes None, ending iteration); method name `recv()` (blocking receive) implies potential blocking until a value arrives or the channel disconnects

**Implementation:** Introduce a stateful iterator/receiver split such as `Receiver<Blocking>` vs `Receiver<NonBlocking>` (or `Iter<Blocking>` vs `TryIter<NonBlocking>`) using PhantomData; alternatively, use a dedicated newtype `BlockingIter<'a, T>` returned by `iter()` and a `NonBlockingIter<'a, T>` returned by `try_iter()`, making the blocking vs non-blocking protocol explicit at the type level rather than only by which method is called inside next().

---

### 469. Single-associated-mutex protocol (bound to exactly one Mutex for waits)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/sync/condvar/pthread.rs:1-81`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: A Condvar is implicitly required to be used with exactly one mutex for waiting: the first wait binds the condvar to a particular mutex address, and all subsequent waits must use that same mutex, otherwise the code panics. This is enforced with an Atomic address comparison in verify(), but the API surface still allows passing any &Mutex to wait/wait_timeout and only detects misuse at runtime.

**Evidence**:

```rust
use crate::sys::sync::{Mutex, OnceBox};
use crate::time::{Duration, Instant};

pub struct Condvar {
    cvar: OnceBox<pal::Condvar>,
    mutex: Atomic<usize>,
}

impl Condvar {
    pub const fn new() -> Condvar {
        Condvar { cvar: OnceBox::new(), mutex: AtomicUsize::new(0) }
    }

    #[inline]
    fn get(&self) -> Pin<&pal::Condvar> {
        self.cvar.get_or_init(|| {
            let mut cvar = Box::pin(pal::Condvar::new());
            // SAFETY: we only call `init` once per `pal::Condvar`, namely here.
            unsafe { cvar.as_mut().init() };
            cvar
        })
    }

    #[inline]
    fn verify(&self, mutex: Pin<&pal::Mutex>) {
        let addr = ptr::from_ref::<pal::Mutex>(&mutex).addr();
        // Relaxed is okay here because we never read through `self.mutex`, and only use it to
        // compare addresses.
        match self.mutex.compare_exchange(0, addr, Relaxed, Relaxed) {
            Ok(_) => {}               // Stored the address
            Err(n) if n == addr => {} // Lost a race to store the same address
            _ => panic!("attempted to use a condition variable with two mutexes"),
        }
    }

    #[inline]
    pub fn notify_one(&self) {
        // SAFETY: we called `init` above.
        unsafe { self.get().notify_one() }
    }

    #[inline]
    pub fn notify_all(&self) {
        // SAFETY: we called `init` above.
        unsafe { self.get().notify_all() }
    }

    #[inline]
    pub unsafe fn wait(&self, mutex: &Mutex) {
        // SAFETY: the caller guarantees that the lock is owned, thus the mutex
        // must have been initialized already.
        let mutex = unsafe { mutex.pal.get_unchecked() };
        self.verify(mutex);
        // SAFETY: we called `init` above, we verified that this condition
        // variable is only used with `mutex` and the caller guarantees that
        // `mutex` is locked by the current thread.
        unsafe { self.get().wait(mutex) }
    }

    pub unsafe fn wait_timeout(&self, mutex: &Mutex, dur: Duration) -> bool {
        // SAFETY: the caller guarantees that the lock is owned, thus the mutex
        // must have been initialized already.
        let mutex = unsafe { mutex.pal.get_unchecked() };
        self.verify(mutex);

        if pal::Condvar::PRECISE_TIMEOUT {
            // SAFETY: we called `init` above, we verified that this condition
            // variable is only used with `mutex` and the caller guarantees that
            // `mutex` is locked by the current thread.
            unsafe { self.get().wait_timeout(mutex, dur) }
        } else {
            // Timeout reports are not reliable, so do the check ourselves.
            let now = Instant::now();
            // SAFETY: we called `init` above, we verified that this condition
            // variable is only used with `mutex` and the caller guarantees that
            // `mutex` is locked by the current thread.
            let woken = unsafe { self.get().wait_timeout(mutex, dur) };
            woken || now.elapsed() < dur
        }
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Condvar

**States:** Unbound (no mutex address recorded), BoundTo(M) (recorded mutex address must match on every wait)

**Transitions:**
- Unbound -> BoundTo(M) via verify() called from wait()/wait_timeout()
- BoundTo(M) -> BoundTo(M) via verify() when called again with same mutex
- BoundTo(M) -> panic via verify() when called with a different mutex

**Evidence:** field `mutex: Atomic<usize>` stores a mutex address (0 meaning 'unbound'); method `fn verify(&self, mutex: Pin<&pal::Mutex>)` computes `addr` and does `compare_exchange(0, addr, ...)`; panic in verify(): `panic!("attempted to use a condition variable with two mutexes")`; wait()/wait_timeout() both call `self.verify(mutex);` before calling into pal::Condvar

**Implementation:** Introduce a bound wrapper/capability such as `struct BoundCondvar<'a> { c: &'a Condvar, m: &'a Mutex }` (or `CondvarBound<'a, M>`), created by a binding step the first time (or at construction). Then expose `BoundCondvar::wait(&self, guard: MutexGuard<'a>)` / `wait_timeout(...)`, making it impossible to call wait with an unrelated mutex without first obtaining the binding token.

### 721. Split iterator protocol (streaming read with delimiter-stripping semantics)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/io/mod.rs:1-28`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: typestate

**Description**: Split encodes an implicit streaming protocol over an underlying BufRead. Each next() call advances the underlying reader by calling read_until(delim, ...). Ok(0) is treated as a terminal EOF state (Finished) and thereafter the iterator is expected to keep returning None. Ok(n>0) yields a chunk and then conditionally strips the trailing delimiter byte if present. Err(e) yields an error item; the API does not encode whether iteration should be considered terminal after an error (it continues to allow next() calls), leaving post-error behavior to the underlying BufRead and caller expectations. None of these interaction/terminal-state expectations are represented in the type system; they are enforced by Iterator conventions and by runtime branching on the read_until result.

**Evidence**:

```rust
// Note: Other parts of this module contain: struct Guard, impl Drop for Guard < '_ > (1 methods); struct IoSliceMut, 1 free function(s), impl Send for IoSliceMut < 'a > (0 methods), impl Sync for IoSliceMut < 'a > (0 methods), impl IoSliceMut < 'a > (4 methods), impl Deref for IoSliceMut < 'a > (1 methods), impl DerefMut for IoSliceMut < 'a > (1 methods); struct IoSlice, 1 free function(s), impl Send for IoSlice < 'a > (0 methods), impl Sync for IoSlice < 'a > (0 methods), impl IoSlice < 'a > (4 methods), impl Deref for IoSlice < 'a > (1 methods); struct Chain, impl Chain < T , U > (3 methods), impl Read for Chain < T , U > (5 methods), impl BufRead for Chain < T , U > (3 methods), impl SizeHint for Chain < T , U > (2 methods); struct Take, impl Take < T > (6 methods), impl Read for Take < T > (2 methods), impl BufRead for Take < T > (2 methods), impl SizeHint for Take < T > (2 methods), impl Seek for Take < T > (4 methods); struct Bytes, impl Iterator for Bytes < R > (2 methods); struct Lines, impl Iterator for Lines < B > (1 methods); enum SeekFrom; trait Read, trait Write, trait Seek, trait BufRead, trait SpecReadByte, trait SizeHint, 13 free function(s), impl SpecReadByte for R (1 methods), impl SizeHint for T (2 methods), impl SizeHint for & mut T (2 methods), impl SizeHint for Box < T > (2 methods), impl SizeHint for & [u8] (2 methods)

/// [`split`]: BufRead::split
#[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Split<B> {
    buf: B,
    delim: u8,
}

#[stable(feature = "rust1", since = "1.0.0")]
impl<B: BufRead> Iterator for Split<B> {
    type Item = Result<Vec<u8>>;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Result<Vec<u8>>> {
        let mut buf = Vec::new();
        match self.buf.read_until(self.delim, &mut buf) {
            Ok(0) => None,
            Ok(_n) => {
                if buf[buf.len() - 1] == self.delim {
                    buf.pop();
                }
                Some(Ok(buf))
            }
            Err(e) => Some(Err(e)),
        }
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Split<B>

**States:** Ready (more input may be available), Finished (EOF reached), Error (I/O error observed)

**Transitions:**
- Ready -> Finished via next() when self.buf.read_until(...) returns Ok(0)
- Ready -> Ready via next() when self.buf.read_until(...) returns Ok(_n>0)
- Ready -> Error via next() when self.buf.read_until(...) returns Err(e)
- Error -> (Ready|Error|Finished) via subsequent next() calls (not type-restricted)

**Evidence:** field `buf: B` stores the underlying reader whose internal position/state is advanced by iteration; field `delim: u8` configures the delimiter protocol used by next(); method `next()`: `match self.buf.read_until(self.delim, &mut buf)` defines the state transitions; branch `Ok(0) => None` treats 0 bytes read as terminal EOF for the iterator; branch `Ok(_n) => ... if buf[buf.len() - 1] == self.delim { buf.pop(); }` encodes delimiter-stripping semantics as part of the protocol; branch `Err(e) => Some(Err(e))` exposes an error state without making it terminal at the type level

**Implementation:** Expose a state-parameterized iterator wrapper, e.g. `Split<B, S>` with `S = Running|Done`, where `next` on `Running` can return either an item and stay `Running` or transition (by returning `Split<B, Done>` or an enum wrapper) to `Done` when `Ok(0)` occurs. Alternatively, return a dedicated enum like `SplitStep { Item(Result<Vec<u8>>), Done }` from a `step(self)` method to encode completion explicitly. (Error-terminal behavior could also be modeled with a separate `Errored` state if desired.)

### 930. each_addr address-iteration protocol (Resolve -> Try each SocketAddr -> Return first success / last error / NO_ADDRESSES)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/net/connection/mod.rs:1-61`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: newtype

**Description**: each_addr relies on an implicit multi-step protocol: it first resolves `addr` via `ToSocketAddrs`, then iterates through the resulting `SocketAddr` list, calling `f` on each until one succeeds. If all attempts fail, it returns the *last* error produced by `f`. If resolution yields zero addresses, it returns a sentinel `Error::NO_ADDRESSES`. None of these states (non-empty vs empty resolution result; 'keep trying' vs 'stop on success'; 'use last error' fallback) are represented in the type system—everything is encoded via control flow and an `Option` accumulator.

**Evidence**:

```rust
cfg_select! {
    any(
        all(target_family = "unix", not(target_os = "l4re")),
        target_os = "windows",
        target_os = "hermit",
        all(target_os = "wasi", any(target_env = "p2", target_env = "p3")),
        target_os = "solid_asp3",
    ) => {
        mod socket;
        pub use socket::*;
    }
    all(target_vendor = "fortanix", target_env = "sgx") => {
        mod sgx;
        pub use sgx::*;
    }
    all(target_os = "wasi", target_env = "p1") => {
        mod wasip1;
        pub use wasip1::*;
    }
    target_os = "motor" => {
        mod motor;
        pub use motor::*;
    }
    target_os = "xous" => {
        mod xous;
        pub use xous::*;
    }
    target_os = "uefi" => {
        mod uefi;
        pub use uefi::*;
    }
    _ => {
        mod unsupported;
        pub use unsupported::*;
    }
}

#[cfg_attr(
    // Make sure that this is used on some platforms at least.
    not(any(target_os = "linux", target_os = "windows")),
    allow(dead_code)
)]
fn each_addr<A: crate::net::ToSocketAddrs, F, T>(addr: A, mut f: F) -> crate::io::Result<T>
where
    F: FnMut(&crate::net::SocketAddr) -> crate::io::Result<T>,
{
    use crate::io::Error;

    let mut last_err = None;
    for addr in addr.to_socket_addrs()? {
        match f(&addr) {
            Ok(l) => return Ok(l),
            Err(e) => last_err = Some(e),
        }
    }

    match last_err {
        Some(err) => Err(err),
        None => Err(Error::NO_ADDRESSES),
    }
}

```

**Entity:** each_addr<A, F, T>(addr: A, f: F)

**States:** Unresolved, ResolvedNonEmpty, ResolvedEmpty, TryingAddrs, Succeeded, FailedWithLastErr, FailedNoAddresses

**Transitions:**
- Unresolved -> ResolvedNonEmpty via `addr.to_socket_addrs()?` yielding at least one item
- Unresolved -> ResolvedEmpty via `addr.to_socket_addrs()?` yielding zero items
- ResolvedNonEmpty -> TryingAddrs via `for addr in ...`
- TryingAddrs -> Succeeded via `Ok(l) => return Ok(l)`
- TryingAddrs -> FailedWithLastErr via exhausting loop with `last_err = Some(e)` set
- ResolvedEmpty -> FailedNoAddresses via `None => Err(Error::NO_ADDRESSES)`

**Evidence:** `for addr in addr.to_socket_addrs()? { ... }` encodes the 'resolve then iterate' protocol; `let mut last_err = None;` and `Err(e) => last_err = Some(e),` encodes 'remember last error' state; `Ok(l) => return Ok(l),` encodes early-exit on first success; `match last_err { Some(err) => Err(err), None => Err(Error::NO_ADDRESSES) }` encodes the 'no addresses' vs 'all failed' distinction; `Error::NO_ADDRESSES` constant explicitly names the empty-resolution invariant

**Implementation:** Introduce a helper that makes the 'non-empty address list' an explicit type, e.g. `struct NonEmptyAddrs(Vec<SocketAddr>); impl TryFrom<A> for NonEmptyAddrs` returning `Error::NO_ADDRESSES` if empty. Then `each_addr` can accept `NonEmptyAddrs` (or split into `resolve_nonempty(addr) -> Result<NonEmptyAddrs>` and `try_each(nonempty, f)`), eliminating the implicit 'empty vs non-empty' state from the control flow and making call sites statically distinguish the two phases.

### 506. Condvar wait protocol requires holding the matching SRW mutex (Locked / Unlocked)

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/sys/sync/condvar/windows7.rs:1-47`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: capability

**Description**: Condvar::wait and Condvar::wait_timeout are unsafe because they rely on an external protocol: the caller must pass a Mutex that is currently locked (by the current thread) and is the correct mutex associated with the condition variable wait. The OS primitive SleepConditionVariableSRW expects the SRW lock to be held and will atomically release it while waiting and re-acquire it before returning. None of this locking state (locked/unlocked, ownership, or association between this Condvar and the Mutex) is represented in the types: the API takes a bare &Mutex, so a caller can pass an unlocked mutex, the wrong mutex, or violate ownership rules, leading to UB or logic bugs; correctness is enforced only by the `unsafe` on the method.

**Evidence**:

```rust
use crate::sys::sync::{Mutex, mutex};
use crate::time::Duration;

pub struct Condvar {
    inner: UnsafeCell<c::CONDITION_VARIABLE>,
}

unsafe impl Send for Condvar {}
unsafe impl Sync for Condvar {}

impl Condvar {
    #[inline]
    pub const fn new() -> Condvar {
        Condvar { inner: UnsafeCell::new(c::CONDITION_VARIABLE_INIT) }
    }

    #[inline]
    pub unsafe fn wait(&self, mutex: &Mutex) {
        let r = c::SleepConditionVariableSRW(self.inner.get(), mutex::raw(mutex), c::INFINITE, 0);
        debug_assert!(r != 0);
    }

    pub unsafe fn wait_timeout(&self, mutex: &Mutex, dur: Duration) -> bool {
        let r = c::SleepConditionVariableSRW(
            self.inner.get(),
            mutex::raw(mutex),
            crate::sys::pal::dur2timeout(dur),
            0,
        );
        if r == 0 {
            debug_assert_eq!(crate::sys::io::errno() as usize, c::ERROR_TIMEOUT as usize);
            false
        } else {
            true
        }
    }

    #[inline]
    pub fn notify_one(&self) {
        unsafe { c::WakeConditionVariable(self.inner.get()) }
    }

    #[inline]
    pub fn notify_all(&self) {
        unsafe { c::WakeAllConditionVariable(self.inner.get()) }
    }
}

```

**Entity:** Condvar

**States:** MutexUnlocked, MutexLockedByCurrentThread

**Transitions:**
- MutexLockedByCurrentThread -> (temporarily releases during wait) -> MutexLockedByCurrentThread via Condvar::wait()
- MutexLockedByCurrentThread -> (temporarily releases during wait) -> MutexLockedByCurrentThread via Condvar::wait_timeout()

**Evidence:** field `inner: UnsafeCell<c::CONDITION_VARIABLE>` indicates interior mutability for an OS synchronization primitive accessed through shared references; method signature `pub unsafe fn wait(&self, mutex: &Mutex)` exposes that safety depends on caller-maintained mutex state, not encoded in types; method body calls `c::SleepConditionVariableSRW(self.inner.get(), mutex::raw(mutex), c::INFINITE, 0)` — Windows API requires the SRW lock be held and will release/reacquire it around the wait; method signature `pub unsafe fn wait_timeout(&self, mutex: &Mutex, dur: Duration) -> bool` has the same dependence on the mutex state

**Implementation:** Require a lock capability type rather than `&Mutex`, e.g., `fn wait<'a>(&self, guard: MutexGuard<'a>) -> MutexGuard<'a>` or `fn wait(&self, locked: &mut LockedMutex)` where `LockedMutex` is only constructible by locking. This encodes 'must be locked by current thread' at compile time and makes the release/reacquire semantics explicit in the API (consume and return the guard).

---

### 1019. SO_PASSCRED configuration protocol (PasscredDisabled / PasscredEnabled) for receiving credentials

**Location**: `/data/rust/library/std/src/os/net/linux_ext/socket.rs:1-66`

**Confidence**: high

**Suggested Pattern**: typestate

**Description**: The socket has an implicit runtime configuration state controlled by the SO_PASSCRED option. Callers must enable SO_PASSCRED (set_passcred(true)) before they can expect sending-process credentials to arrive as ancillary data. This dependency is described in docs but not represented in the type system: any code can attempt to receive/interpret credentials regardless of whether SO_PASSCRED is enabled, leading to runtime absence of expected control messages.

**Evidence**:

```rust
//! Linux and Android-specific socket functionality.

use crate::io;
use crate::os::unix::net;
use crate::sealed::Sealed;
use crate::sys::AsInner;

/// Linux-specific functionality for `AF_UNIX` sockets [`UnixDatagram`]
/// and [`UnixStream`].
///
/// [`UnixDatagram`]: net::UnixDatagram
/// [`UnixStream`]: net::UnixStream
#[unstable(feature = "unix_socket_ancillary_data", issue = "76915")]
pub trait UnixSocketExt: Sealed {
    /// Query the current setting of socket option `SO_PASSCRED`.
    #[unstable(feature = "unix_socket_ancillary_data", issue = "76915")]
    fn passcred(&self) -> io::Result<bool>;

    /// Enable or disable socket option `SO_PASSCRED`.
    ///
    /// This option enables the credentials of the sending process to be
    /// received as a control message in [`AncillaryData`].
    ///
    /// [`AncillaryData`]: net::AncillaryData
    ///
    /// # Examples
    ///
    /// ```no_run
    /// #![feature(unix_socket_ancillary_data)]
    /// #[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
    /// use std::os::linux::net::UnixSocketExt;
    /// #[cfg(target_os = "android")]
    /// use std::os::android::net::UnixSocketExt;
    /// use std::os::unix::net::UnixDatagram;
    ///
    /// fn main() -> std::io::Result<()> {
    ///     let sock = UnixDatagram::unbound()?;
    ///     sock.set_passcred(true).expect("set_passcred failed");
    ///     Ok(())
    /// }
    /// ```
    #[unstable(feature = "unix_socket_ancillary_data", issue = "76915")]
    fn set_passcred(&self, passcred: bool) -> io::Result<()>;
}

#[unstable(feature = "unix_socket_ancillary_data", issue = "76915")]
impl UnixSocketExt for net::UnixDatagram {
    fn passcred(&self) -> io::Result<bool> {
        self.as_inner().passcred()
    }

    fn set_passcred(&self, passcred: bool) -> io::Result<()> {
        self.as_inner().set_passcred(passcred)
    }
}

#[unstable(feature = "unix_socket_ancillary_data", issue = "76915")]
impl UnixSocketExt for net::UnixStream {
    fn passcred(&self) -> io::Result<bool> {
        self.as_inner().passcred()
    }

    fn set_passcred(&self, passcred: bool) -> io::Result<()> {
        self.as_inner().set_passcred(passcred)
    }
}

```

**Entity:** net::UnixDatagram / net::UnixStream (via UnixSocketExt)

**States:** PasscredDisabled, PasscredEnabled

**Transitions:**
- PasscredDisabled -> PasscredEnabled via UnixSocketExt::set_passcred(true)
- PasscredEnabled -> PasscredDisabled via UnixSocketExt::set_passcred(false)

**Evidence:** trait method: fn passcred(&self) -> io::Result<bool> queries current SO_PASSCRED setting; trait method: fn set_passcred(&self, passcred: bool) toggles SO_PASSCRED at runtime; doc comment on set_passcred: "This option enables the credentials of the sending process to be received as a control message in AncillaryData" (implies a required configuration step before expecting credentials)

**Implementation:** Introduce a wrapper typestate around UnixDatagram/UnixStream, e.g., struct CredSock<S> { inner: UnixDatagram, _s: PhantomData<S> } with states PasscredOff/PasscredOn. Provide enable_passcred(self) -> io::Result<CredSock<PasscredOn>> and disable_passcred(self) -> io::Result<CredSock<PasscredOff>>; expose credential-aware recv/ancillary parsing APIs only on CredSock<PasscredOn>.
